text.NABRE: For she is fairer than the sun and surpasses every constellation of the stars. Compared to light, she is found more radiant;
text.NRSV: She is more beautiful than the sun, and excels every constellation of the stars. Compared with the light she is found to be superior,
text.DRB: For she is more beautiful than the sun, and above all disposition of the stars, being compared to light she is found the first.

text.KJV: For after this cometh night: but vice shall not prevail against wisdom.
text.NABRE: though night supplants light, wickedness does not prevail over Wisdom.
text.NRSV: for it is succeeded by the night, but against wisdom evil does not prevail.
text.DRB: For night succeedeth to it, but malice overcometh not wisdom.

text.KJV: Wisdom reacheth from one end to another mightily: and sweetly doth she order all things.
text.NABRE: Chapter 8 Indeed, she spans the world from end to end mightily and governs all things well.
text.NRSV: 8 and she orders all things well.
text.DRB: She reacheth therefore from end unto end mightily, and disposeth all things sweetly.

text.KJV: I loved her, and sought her out from my youth, I desired to make her my spouse, and I was a lover of her beauty.
text.NABRE: Wisdom, the Source of Blessings Her I loved and sought after from my youth; I sought to take her for my bride and was enamored of her beauty.
text.NRSV: Solomon’s Love for Wisdom I loved her and sought her from my youth; I desired to take her for my bride, and became enamoured of her beauty.
text.DRB: Her have I loved, and have sought her out from my youth, and have sought to take her for my spouse, and I was made a lover of her beauty.
She glorifieth her nobility, having consociation with God: yea and the Lord of all hath loved her.
text.KJV: In that she is conversant with God, she magnifieth her nobility: yea, the Lord of all things himself loved her.
text.NABRE: She adds to nobility the splendor of companionship with God; even the Ruler of all loved her.
text.NRSV: She glorifies her noble birth by living with God, and the Lord of all loves her.

text.DRB: For she is the mistress of the discipline of God, & the chooser of his works.
text.KJV: For she is privy to the mysteries of the knowledge of God, and a lover of his works.
text.NABRE: For she leads into the understanding of God, and chooses his works.
text.NRSV: For she is an initiate in the knowledge of God, and an associate in his works.

text.DRB: And if riches be desired in life, what is richer than wisdom, which worketh all things?
text.KJV: If riches be a possession to be desired in this life; what is richer than wisdom, that worketh all things?
text.NABRE: If riches are desirable in life, what is richer than Wisdom, who produces all things?
text.NRSV: If riches are a desirable possession in life, what is richer than wisdom, the active cause of all things?

text.DRB: And if understanding do work: who is the worker of those things that are, more than she?
text.KJV: And if prudence work; who of all that are is a more cunning workman than she?
text.NABRE: And if prudence is at work, who in the world is a better artisan than she?
text.NRSV: And if understanding is effective, who more than she is fashioner of what exists?

text.KJV: And if a man love righteousness her labours are virtues: for she teacheth temperance and prudence, justice and fortitude: which are such things, as men can have nothing more profitable in their life.
text.NABRE: Or if one loves righteousness, whose works are virtues, She teaches moderation and prudence, righteousness and fortitude, and nothing in life is more useful than these.
text.NRSV: And if anyone loves righteousness, her labours are virtues; for she teaches self-control and prudence, justice and courage; nothing in life is more profitable for mortals than these.
text.DRB: And if a man love justice: her labours have great virtues: for she teacheth sobriety, and prudence, and justice, and strength, than the which nothing is more profitable in life to men.

And if a man desire multitude of knowledge, she knoweth things past, & conjectureth of things to come: she knoweth the subtilties of words, and the solutions of arguments: she knoweth signs and wonders before they be done, and the events of times and ages.
text.KJV: If a man desire much experience, she knoweth things of old, and conjectureth aright what is to come: she knoweth the subtilties of speeches, and can expound dark sentences: she foreseeth signs and wonders, and the events of seasons and times.
text.NABRE: Or again, if one yearns for wide experience, she knows the things of old, and infers the things to come. She understands the turns of phrases and the solutions of riddles; signs and wonders she knows in advance and the outcome of times and ages.
text.NRSV: And if anyone longs for wide experience, she knows the things of old, and infers the things to come; she understands turns of speech and the solutions of riddles; she has foreknowledge of signs and wonders and of the outcome of seasons and times.

text.DRB: I purposed therefore to bring her to me to live together: knowing that she will communicate unto me of good things, and will be a comfort of my cogitation & tediousness.
text.KJV: Therefore I purposed to take her to me to live with me, knowing that she would be a counsellor of good things, and a comfort in cares and grief.
text.NABRE: Wisdom as Solomon’s Counselor and Comfort So I determined to take her to live with me, knowing that she would be my counselor while all was well, and my comfort in care and grief.
text.NRSV: Wisdom Indispensable to Rulers Therefore I determined to take her to live with me, knowing that she would give me good counsel and encouragement in cares and grief.

text.DRB: Ishall have for her sake glory with the multitudes and honour with the ancient being young:
text.KJV: For her sake I shall have estimation among the multitude, and honour with the elders, though I be young.
text.NABRE: Because of her I have glory among the multitudes, and esteem from the elders, though I am but a youth.
text.NRSV: Because of her I shall have glory among the multitudes and honour in the presence of the elders, though I am young.

text.DRB: and I shall be found sharp in judgement, and in the sight of the mighty I shall be marvelous, and the faces of Princes will marvel at me.
text.KJV: I shall be found of a quick conceit in judgment, and shall be admired in the sight of great men.
text.NABRE: I shall become keen in judgment, and shall be a marvel before rulers.
text.NRSV: I shall be found keen in judgement, and in the sight of rulers I shall be admired.

text.DRB: Holding my peace they shall expect me, and whilst I speak many words, they shall lay their hands on their mouth.
text.KJV: When I hold my tongue, they shall bide my leisure, and when I speak, they shall give good ear unto me: if I talk much, they shall lay their hands upon their mouth.
text.NABRE: They will wait while I am silent and listen when I speak; and when I shall speak the more, they will put their hands upon their mouths.
text.NRSV: When I am silent they will wait for me, and when I speak they will give heed; if I speak at greater length, they will put their hands on their mouths.

text.DRB: Moreover by her I shall have immortality: and I shall leave an eternal memory to them, that shall be after me.
text.KJV: Moreover by the means of her I shall obtain immortality, and leave behind me an everlasting memorial to them that come after me.
text.NABRE: Because of her I shall have immortality and leave to those after me an everlasting memory.
text.NRSV: Because of her I shall have immortality, and leave an everlasting remembrance to those who come after me.

text.DRB: Ishall dispose peoples, and nations shall be subject to me.
text.KJV: I shall set the people in order, and the nations shall be subject unto me.
text.NABRE: I shall govern peoples, and nations will be my subjects—
text.NRSV: I shall govern peoples, and nations will be subject to me;

text.DRB: Horrible kings hearing shall fear me: in the multitude I shall seem good, and in battle strong.
text.KJV: Horrible tyrants shall be afraid, when they do but hear of me; I shall be found good among the multitude, and valiant in war.
text.NABRE: tyrannical princes, hearing of me, will be afraid; in the assembly I shall appear noble, and in war courageous.
text.NRSV: dread monarchs will be afraid of me when they hear of me; among the people I shall show myself capable, and courageous in war.

text.DRB: Entering into my house, I shall rest with her: for her conversation hath no bitterness, nor her company tediousness, but joy and gladness.
text.KJV: After I am come into mine house, I will repose myself with her: for her conversation hath no bitterness; and to live with her hath no sorrow, but mirth and joy.
text.NABRE: Entering my house, I shall take my repose beside her; For association with her involves no bitterness and living with her no grief, but rather joy and gladness.
text.NRSV: When I enter my house, I shall find rest with her; for companionship with her has no bitterness, and life with her has no pain, but gladness and joy.

text.DRB: Thinking these things with myself, and recording in my heart, that immortality is in the kindred of wisdom,
text.KJV: Now when I considered these things in myself, and pondered them in my heart, how that to be allied unto wisdom is immortality;
text.NABRE: Wisdom Is a Gift of God Reflecting on these things, and considering in my heart That immortality lies in kinship with Wisdom,
text.NRSV: When I considered these things inwardly, and pondered in my heart that in kinship with wisdom there is immortality,

text.DRB: and good delectation in her friendship, and in the works of her hands honesty without defect, and wisdom in the disputation of her talk, and glory in the communication of her words: I went about seeking, that I might take her to me.
text.KJV: And great pleasure it is to have her friendship; and in the works of her hands are infinite riches; and in the exercise of conference with her, prudence; and in talking with her, a good report; I went about seeking how to take her to me.
text.NABRE: great delight in love of her, and unfailing riches in the works of her hands; And that in associating with her there is prudence, and fair renown in sharing her discourses, I went about seeking to take her for my own.
text.NRSV: and in friendship with her, pure delight, and in the labours of her hands, unfailing wealth, and in the experience of her company, understanding, and renown in sharing her words, I went about seeking how to get her for myself.

text.DRB: And I was a witty child, and had gotten a good soul.
text.KJV: For I was a witty child, and had a good spirit.
text.NABRE: Now, I was a well-favored child, and I came by a noble nature;
text.NRSV: As a child I was naturally gifted, and a good soul fell to my lot;

text.DRB: And whereas I was more good, I came to a body undefiled.
text.KJV: Yea rather, being good, I came into a body undefiled.
text.NABRE: or rather, being noble, I attained an unblemished body.
text.NRSV: or rather, being good, I entered an undefiled body.

text.DRB: And as I knew that I could not otherwise be continent, unless God gave it, this very thing also was wisdom, to know whose this gift was: I went to our Lord, and besought him, and said from my whole heart:
text.KJV: Nevertheless, when I perceived that I could not otherwise obtain her, except God gave her me; and that was a point of wisdom also to know whose gift she was; I prayed unto the Lord, and besought him, and with my whole heart I said,
text.NABRE: And knowing that I could not otherwise possess her unless God gave it— and this, too, was prudence, to know whose gift she is— I went to the Lord and besought him, and said with all my heart:
text.NRSV: But I perceived that I would not possess wisdom unless God gave her to me— and it was a mark of insight to know whose gift she was— so I appealed to the Lord and implored him, and with my whole heart I said:

text.KJV: O God of my fathers, and Lord of mercy, who hast made all things with thy word,
text.NABRE: Chapter 9 Solomon’s Prayer God of my ancestors, Lord of mercy, you who have made all things by your word
text.NRSV: Solomon’s Prayer for Wisdom 9 who have made all things by your word,
text.DRB: God of my fathers, and Lord of mercy, which madest all things with thy Word,

text.KJV: And ordained man through thy wisdom, that he should have dominion over the creatures which thou hast made,
text.NABRE: And in your wisdom have established humankind to rule the creatures produced by you,
text.NRSV: and by your wisdom have formed humankind to have dominion over the creatures you have made,
text.DRB: and by thy Wisdom didst appoint man, that he should have dominion of the creature, that was made by thee,
that he should dispose the round world in equity and justice, and execute judgement in direction of heart:
text.KJV: And order the world according to equity and righteousness, and execute judgment with an upright heart:
text.NABRE: And to govern the world in holiness and righteousness, and to render judgment in integrity of heart:
text.NRSV: and rule the world in holiness and righteousness, and pronounce judgement in uprightness of soul,

text.KJV: Give me wisdom, that sitteth by thy throne; and reject me not from among thy children:
text.NABRE: Give me Wisdom, the consort at your throne, and do not reject me from among your children;
text.NRSV: give me the wisdom that sits by your throne, and do not reject me from among your servants.
text.DRB: give me wisdom the assistant of thy seats, and repel me not from thy children:
because I am thy servant, and the son of thy handmaid, a weak man and of small time, and less to the understanding of judgement and laws.
text.KJV: For I thy servant and son of thine handmaid am a feeble person, and of a short time, and too young for the understanding of judgment and laws.
text.NABRE: For I am your servant, the child of your maidservant, a man weak and short-lived and lacking in comprehension of judgment and of laws.
text.NRSV: For I am your servant, the son of your servant-girl, a man who is weak and short-lived, with little understanding of judgement and laws;

text.DRB: And if one be perfect among the children of men, and thy wisdom be absent from him, he shall be counted for nothing.
text.KJV: For though a man be never so perfect among the children of men, yet if thy wisdom be not with him, he shall be nothing regarded.
text.NABRE: Indeed, though one be perfect among mortals, if Wisdom, who comes from you, be lacking, that one will count for nothing.
text.NRSV: for even one who is perfect among human beings will be regarded as nothing without the wisdom that comes from you.

text.DRB: Thou hast chosen me king to thy people, and judge of thy sons and daughters:
text.KJV: Thou hast chosen me to be a king of thy people, and a judge of thy sons and daughters:
text.NABRE: You have chosen me king over your people and magistrate over your sons and daughters.
text.NRSV: You have chosen me to be king of your people and to be judge over your sons and daughters.

text.DRB: and badest me build a temple in thy holy mount, and an altar in the city of thy habitation, a similitude of thy holy tabernacle, which thou didst prepare from the beginning:
text.KJV: Thou hast commanded me to build a temple upon thy holy mount, and an altar in the city wherein thou dwellest, a resemblance of the holy tabernacle, which thou hast prepared from the beginning.
text.NABRE: You have bid me build a temple on your holy mountain and an altar in the city that is your dwelling place, a copy of the holy tabernacle which you had established from of old.
text.NRSV: You have given command to build a temple on your holy mountain, and an altar in the city of your habitation, a copy of the holy tent that you prepared from the beginning.

text.KJV: And wisdom was with thee: which knoweth thy works, and was present when thou madest the world, and knew what was acceptable in thy sight, and right in thy commandments.
text.NABRE: Now with you is Wisdom, who knows your works and was present when you made the world; Who understands what is pleasing in your eyes and what is conformable with your commands.
text.NRSV: With you is wisdom, she who knows your works and was present when you made the world; she understands what is pleasing in your sight and what is right according to your commandments.
text.DRB: and thy wisdom with thee, which knew thy works, which then also was present when thou madest the round world, and knew what was pleasing to thine eyes, and what was direct in thy precepts.

text.KJV: O send her out of thy holy heavens, and from the throne of thy glory, that being present she may labour with me, that I may know what is pleasing unto thee.
text.NABRE: Send her forth from your holy heavens and from your glorious throne dispatch her That she may be with me and work with me, that I may know what is pleasing to you.
text.NRSV: Send her forth from the holy heavens, and from the throne of your glory send her, that she may labour at my side, and that I may learn what is pleasing to you.
text.DRB: Send her from thy holy heavens, and from the seat of thy greatness, that she may be with me, and may labour with me, that I may know what is acceptable with thee:
for she knoweth all things & understandeth, and shall conduct me in my works soberly, & shall keep me with her might.
text.KJV: For she knoweth and understandeth all things, and she shall lead me soberly in my doings, and preserve me in her power.
text.NABRE: For she knows and understands all things, and will guide me prudently in my affairs and safeguard me by her glory;
text.NRSV: For she knows and understands all things, and she will guide me wisely in my actions and guard me with her glory.

text.DRB: And my works shall be acceptable, and I shall govern thy people justly, and shall be worthy of the seats of my father.
text.KJV: So shall my works be acceptable, and then shall I judge thy people righteously, and be worthy to sit in my father’s seat.
text.NABRE: Thus my deeds will be acceptable, and I will judge your people justly and be worthy of my father’s throne.
text.NRSV: Then my works will be acceptable, and I shall judge your people justly, and shall be worthy of the throne of my father.

text.KJV: For what man is he that can know the counsel of God? or who can think what the will of the Lord is?
text.NABRE: For who knows God’s counsel, or who can conceive what the Lord intends?
text.NRSV: For who can learn the counsel of God? Or who can discern what the Lord wills?
text.DRB: For who of men is able to know the counsel of God? Or who can think what God will?
For the cogitations of mortal men be fearful, and our providences uncertain.
text.KJV: For the thoughts of mortal men are miserable, and our devices are but uncertain.
text.NABRE: For the deliberations of mortals are timid, and uncertain our plans.
text.NRSV: For the reasoning of mortals is worthless, and our designs are likely to fail;

text.KJV: For the corruptible body presseth down the soul, and the earthy tabernacle weigheth down the mind that museth upon many things.
text.NABRE: For the corruptible body burdens the soul and the earthly tent weighs down the mind with its many concerns.
text.NRSV: for a perishable body weighs down the soul, and this earthy tent burdens the thoughtful mind.
text.DRB: For the body that is corrupted burdeneth the soul, and the earthly habitation presseth down the understanding that thinketh many things.
And we do hardly conjecture the things that are in the earth: and the things that are in sight, we find with labour. But the things that are in the heavens who shall search out?
text.KJV: And hardly do we guess aright at things that are upon earth, and with labour do we find the things that are before us: but the things that are in heaven who hath searched out?
text.NABRE: Scarcely can we guess the things on earth, and only with difficulty grasp what is at hand; but things in heaven, who can search them out?
text.NRSV: We can hardly guess at what is on earth, and what is at hand we find with labour; but who has traced out what is in the heavens?

text.DRB: And thy sense who shall know, unless thou give wisdom, and send thy holy Spirit from on high:
text.KJV: And thy counsel who hath known, except thou give wisdom, and send thy Holy Spirit from above?
text.NABRE: Or who can know your counsel, unless you give Wisdom and send your holy spirit from on high?
text.NRSV: Who has learned your counsel, unless you have given wisdom and sent your holy spirit from on high?

text.DRB: and so the paths of them that are on the earth, may be corrected, and men learn the things that please thee?
text.KJV: For so the ways of them which lived on the earth were reformed, and men were taught the things that are pleasing unto thee, and were saved through wisdom.
text.NABRE: Thus were the paths of those on earth made straight, and people learned what pleases you, and were saved by Wisdom.
text.NRSV: And thus the paths of those on earth were set right, and people were taught what pleases you, and were saved by wisdom.’

text.DRB: For by wisdom they were healed, whosoever have pleased thee o Lord from the beginning.

text.KJV: She preserved the first formed father of the world, that was created alone, and brought him out of his fall,
text.NABRE: Chapter 10 Wisdom Preserves Her Followers She preserved the first-formed father of the world when he alone had been created; And she raised him up from his fall,
text.NRSV: The Work of Wisdom from Adam to Moses 10 she delivered him from his transgression,
text.DRB: She kept him, that was first made of God father of the world, when he was created alone,

text.KJV: And gave him power to rule all things.
text.NABRE: and gave him power to rule all things.
text.NRSV: and gave him strength to rule all things.
text.DRB: and she brought him out of his sin, and gave him power to contain all things.
After the unjust departed in his anger from her, by the fury of brother's manslaughter he perished.
text.KJV: But when the unrighteous went away from her in his anger, he perished also in the fury wherewith he murdered his brother.
text.NABRE: But when an unrighteous man withdrew from her in his anger, he perished through his fratricidal wrath.
text.NRSV: But when an unrighteous man departed from her in his anger, he perished because in rage he killed his brother.

text.KJV: For whose cause the earth being drowned with the flood, wisdom again preserved it, and directed the course of the righteous in a piece of wood of small value.
text.NABRE: When on his account the earth was flooded, Wisdom again saved it, piloting the righteous man on frailest wood.
text.NRSV: When the earth was flooded because of him, wisdom again saved it, steering the righteous man by a paltry piece of wood.
text.DRB: For whose cause, when water destroyed the earth, wisdom healed it again, governing the just by contemptible wood.

text.KJV: Moreover, the nations in their wicked conspiracy being confounded, she found out the righteous, and preserved him blameless unto God, and kept him strong against his tender compassion toward his son.
text.NABRE: She, when the nations were sunk in universal wickedness, knew the righteous man, kept him blameless before God, and preserved him resolute against pity for his child.
text.NRSV: Wisdom also, when the nations in wicked agreement had been put to confusion, recognized the righteous man and preserved him blameless before God, and kept him strong in the face of his compassion for his child.
text.DRB: She even in the consent of wickedness, when the nations had confederated themselves, knew the just, and preserved him without blame to God, and in his son's mercy kept the strong.

text.KJV: When the ungodly perished, she delivered the righteous man, who fled from the fire which fell down upon the five cities.
text.NABRE: She rescued a righteous man from among the wicked who were being destroyed, when he fled as fire descended upon the Pentapolis—
text.NRSV: Wisdom rescued a righteous man when the ungodly were perishing; he escaped the fire that descended on the Five Cities.
text.DRB: She delivered the just, flying from the impious that perished, when the fire came down upon Pentapolis:
to whom for a witness of their wickedness the desert land standeth smoking, and trees having fruits at uncertain season; and the memory of an incredulous soul a standing pillar of salt.
text.KJV: Of whose wickedness even to this day the waste land that smoketh is a testimony, and plants bearing fruit that never come to ripeness: and a standing pillar of salt is a monument of an unbelieving soul.
text.NABRE: Where as a testimony to its wickedness, even yet there remain a smoking desert, Plants bearing fruit that never ripens, and the tomb of a disbelieving soul, a standing pillar of salt.
text.NRSV: Evidence of their wickedness still remains: a continually smoking waste-land, plants bearing fruit that does not ripen, and a pillar of salt standing as a monument to an unbelieving soul.

text.DRB: For pretermitting wisdom they did not only slip in this, that they were ignorant of good things, but they left also unto men a memory of their foolishness, that in those things, in which they sinned in, they could not be hid neither.
text.KJV: For regarding not wisdom, they gat not only this hurt, that they knew not the things which were good; but also left behind them to the world a memorial of their foolishness: so that in the things wherein they offended they could not so much as be hid.
text.NABRE: For those who forsook Wisdom not only were deprived of knowledge of the good, But also left the world a memorial of their folly, so that they could not even be hidden in their fall.
text.NRSV: For because they passed wisdom by, they not only were hindered from recognizing the good, but also left for humankind a reminder of their folly, so that their failures could never go unnoticed.

text.DRB: But wisdom hath delivered them that observe her from sorrows.
text.KJV: But wisdom delivered from pain those that attended upon her.
text.NABRE: But Wisdom rescued from tribulations those who served her.
text.NRSV: Wisdom rescued from troubles those who served her.

text.KJV: When the righteous fled from his brother’s wrath she guided him in right paths, shewed him the kingdom of God, and gave him knowledge of holy things, made him rich in his travels, and multiplied the fruit of his labours.
text.NABRE: She, when a righteous man fled from his brother’s anger, guided him in right ways, Showed him the kingdom of God and gave him knowledge of holy things; She prospered him in his labors and made abundant the fruit of his works,
text.NRSV: When a righteous man fled from his brother’s wrath, she guided him on straight paths; she showed him the kingdom of God, and gave him knowledge of holy things; she prospered him in his labours, and increased the fruit of his toil.
text.DRB: And the just flying his brother's wrath, she conducted by the right ways, and shewed him the kingdom of God, and gave him the knowledge of the holy, did honest him in labours, and accomplished his labours.
In the fraud of the circumventers of him she was present with him, and made him honourable.
text.KJV: In the covetousness of such as oppressed him she stood by him, and made him rich.
text.NABRE: Stood by him against the greed of his defrauders, and enriched him;
text.NRSV: When his oppressors were covetous, she stood by him and made him rich.

text.DRB: She kept him from the enemies, and from seducers she defended him, and gave him a strong fight, that he might overcome, and know that wisdom is mightier than all.
text.KJV: She defended him from his enemies, and kept him safe from those that lay in wait, and in a sore conflict she gave him the victory; that he might know that goodness is stronger than all.
text.NABRE: She preserved him from foes, and secured him against ambush, And she gave him the prize for his hard struggle that he might know that devotion to God is mightier than all else.
text.NRSV: She protected him from his enemies, and kept him safe from those who lay in wait for him; in his arduous contest she gave him the victory, so that he might learn that godliness is more powerful than anything else.

text.DRB: She forsook not the just being sold, but delivered him from sinners: and she went down with him into the pit,
text.KJV: When the righteous was sold, she forsook him not, but delivered him from sin: she went down with him into the pit,
text.NABRE: She did not abandon a righteous man when he was sold, but rescued him from sin.
text.NRSV: When a righteous man was sold, wisdom did not desert him, but delivered him from sin. She descended with him into the dungeon,

text.DRB: and in bands left him not, till she brought him the sceptre of a Kingdom, and might against them that oppressed him: and shewed them to be liars, that spotted him, and gave him eternal glory.
text.KJV: And left him not in bonds, till she brought him the sceptre of the kingdom, and power against those that oppressed him: as for them that had accused him, she shewed them to be liars, and gave him perpetual glory.
text.NABRE: She went down with him into the dungeon, and did not desert him in his bonds, Until she brought him the scepter of royalty and authority over his oppressors, Proved false those who had defamed him, and gave him eternal glory.
text.NRSV: and when he was in prison she did not leave him, until she brought him the sceptre of a kingdom and authority over his masters. Those who accused him she showed to be false, and she gave him everlasting honour.

text.DRB: The just people, and seed without blame she delivered from the nations, that oppressed them.
text.KJV: She delivered the righteous people and blameless seed from the nation that oppressed them.
text.NABRE: The holy people and their blameless descendants—it was she who rescued them from the nation that oppressed them.
text.NRSV: Wisdom Led the Israelites out of Egypt A holy people and blameless race wisdom delivered from a nation of oppressors.

text.DRB: She entered into the soul of the servant of God, and stood against dreadful kings in wonders and signs.
text.KJV: She entered into the soul of the servant of the Lord, and withstood dreadful kings in wonders and signs;
text.NABRE: She entered the soul of the Lord’s servant, and withstood fearsome kings with signs and wonders;
text.NRSV: She entered the soul of a servant of the Lord, and withstood dread kings with wonders and signs.

text.DRB: And she rendered to the just the hope of their labours, and conducted them in a marvelous way: and she was unto them for a cover in the day, and for the light of stars by night:
text.KJV: Rendered to the righteous a reward of their labours, guided them in a marvellous way, and was unto them for a cover by day, and a light of stars in the night season;
text.NABRE: she gave the holy ones the reward of their labors, Conducted them by a wondrous road, became a shelter for them by day a starry flame by night.
text.NRSV: She gave to holy people the reward of their labours; she guided them along a marvellous way, and became a shelter to them by day, and a starry flame through the night.

text.DRB: and she transported them through the Red sea, and carried them over through a great water.
text.KJV: Brought them through the Red sea, and led them through much water:
text.NABRE: She took them across the Red Sea and brought them through the deep waters.
text.NRSV: She brought them over the Red Sea, and led them through deep waters;

text.DRB: But their enemies she drowned in the sea, and from the depth of hell she brought them out. Therefore the just took the spoils of the impious,
text.KJV: But she drowned their enemies, and cast them up out of the bottom of the deep.
text.NABRE: Their enemies she overwhelmed, and churned them up from the bottom of the depths.
text.NRSV: but she drowned their enemies, and cast them up from the depths of the sea.

text.DRB: and they sang thy holy name o Lord, and thy victorious hand they praised together:
text.KJV: Therefore the righteous spoiled the ungodly, and praised thy holy name, O Lord, and magnified with one accord thine hand, that fought for them.
text.NABRE: Therefore the righteous despoiled the wicked; and they sang of your holy name, Lord, and praised in unison your conquering hand,
text.NRSV: Therefore the righteous plundered the ungodly; they sang hymns, O Lord, to your holy name, and praised with one accord your defending hand;

text.DRB: because wisdom hath opened the mouth of the dumb, and the tongues of infants she hath made eloquent.
text.KJV: For wisdom opened the mouth of the dumb, and made the tongues of them that cannot speak eloquent.
text.NABRE: Because Wisdom opened the mouths of the mute, and gave ready speech to infants.
text.NRSV: for wisdom opened the mouths of those who were mute, and made the tongues of infants speak clearly.

text.KJV: She prospered their works in the hand of the holy prophet.
text.NABRE: Chapter 11 She prospered their affairs through the holy prophet.
text.NRSV: Wisdom Led the Israelites through the Desert 11
text.DRB: She directed their works in the hands of a holy Prophet.

text.KJV: They went through the wilderness that was not inhabited, and pitched tents in places where there lay no way.
text.NABRE: III. Special Providence of God During the Exodus Introduction They journeyed through the uninhabited desert, and in lonely places they pitched their tents;
text.NRSV: They journeyed through an uninhabited wilderness, and pitched their tents in untrodden places.
text.DRB: They made a journey through the deserts, that were not inhabited: and in desert places they pitched cottages.
They stood against the adversaries, and revenged themselves of the enemies.
text.KJV: They stood against their enemies, and were avenged of their adversaries.
text.NABRE: they withstood enemies and warded off their foes.
text.NRSV: They withstood their enemies and fought off their foes.

text.DRB: They thirsted, and invocated thee, and water was given them out of a most high rock, and quenching of their thirst out of the hard stone.
text.KJV: When they were thirsty, they called upon thee, and water was given them out of the flinty rock, and their thirst was quenched out of the hard stone.
text.NABRE: When they thirsted, they called upon you, and water was given them from the sheer rock, a quenching of their thirst from the hard stone.
text.NRSV: When they were thirsty, they called upon you, and water was given them out of flinty rock, and from hard stone a remedy for their thirst.

text.DRB: For by the things whereby their enemies suffered punishment, for defect of their drink, and therein, when the children of Israel abounded, they did rejoice:
text.KJV: For by what things their enemies were punished, by the same they in their need were benefited.
text.NABRE: For by the things through which their foes were punished they in their need were benefited.
text.NRSV: For through the very things by which their enemies were punished, they themselves received benefit in their need.

text.DRB: by these things, when others lacked the same, it went well with them.
text.KJV: For instead of a perpetual running river troubled with foul blood,
text.NABRE: First Example: Water Punishes the Egyptians and Benefits the Israelites Instead of a river’s perennial source, troubled with impure blood
text.NRSV: Instead of the fountain of an ever-flowing river, stirred up and defiled with blood

text.DRB: For in stead of the fountain of an everlasting river, thou gavest man's blood to the unjust.
text.KJV: For a manifest reproof of that commandment, whereby the infants were slain, thou gavest unto them abundance of water by a means which they hoped not for:
text.NABRE: as a rebuke to the decree for the slaying of infants, You gave them abundant water beyond their hope,
text.NRSV: in rebuke for the decree to kill the infants, you gave them abundant water unexpectedly,

text.DRB: Who when they were diminished in the destruction of the murdered infants, thou gavest them aboundant water unlooked for:
text.KJV: Declaring by that thirst then how thou hadst punished their adversaries.
text.NABRE: after you had shown by the thirst they experienced how you punished their adversaries.
text.NRSV: showing by their thirst at that time how you punished their enemies.

text.DRB: shewing by the thirst, that then was, how thou didst exalt thine, & didst kill their adversaries.
text.KJV: For when they were tried albeit but in mercy chastised, they knew how the ungodly were judged in wrath and tormented, thirsting in another manner than the just.
text.NABRE: For when they had been tried, though only mildly chastised, they recognized how the wicked, condemned in anger, were being tormented.
text.NRSV: For when they were tried, though they were being disciplined in mercy, they learned how the ungodly were tormented when judged in wrath.

text.DRB: For when they were tempted, and indeed with mercy taking discipline, they knew how the impious being judged with wrath did suffer torments.
text.KJV: For these thou didst admonish and try, as a father: but the other, as a severe king, thou didst condemn and punish.
text.NABRE: You tested your own people, admonishing them as a father; but as a stern king you probed and condemned the wicked.
text.NRSV: For you tested them as a parent does in warning, but you examined the ungodly as a stern king does in condemnation.

text.DRB: These certes admonishing as a father thou didst prove: but them examining as a hard king thou didst condemn.
text.KJV: Whether they were absent or present, they were vexed alike.
text.NABRE: Those near and far were equally afflicted:
text.NRSV: Whether absent or present, they were equally distressed,

text.DRB: For the absent and the present were tormented alike.
text.KJV: For a double grief came upon them, and a groaning for the remembrance of things past.
text.NABRE: for a twofold grief took hold of them and a groaning at the remembrance of the ones who had departed.
text.NRSV: for a twofold grief possessed them, and a groaning at the memory of what had occurred.

text.DRB: For double tediousness had taken them, and sighing with the memory of good things past.
text.KJV: For when they heard by their own punishments the other to be benefited, they had some feeling of the Lord.
text.NABRE: For when they heard that the cause of their own torments was a benefit to these others, they recognized the Lord.
text.NRSV: For when they heard that through their own punishments the righteous had received benefit, they perceived it was the Lord’s doing.

text.DRB: For when they understood by their punishment that it went well with them, they remembered our Lord, marveling at the end of the event.
text.KJV: For whom they respected with scorn, when he was long before thrown out at the casting forth of the infants, him in the end, when they saw what came to pass, they admired.
text.NABRE: For though they had mocked and rejected him who had been cast out and abandoned long ago, in the final outcome, they marveled at him, since their thirst proved unlike that of the righteous.
text.NRSV: For though they had mockingly rejected him who long before had been cast out and exposed, at the end of the events they marvelled at him, when they felt thirst in a different way from the righteous.

text.DRB: For whom before they derided, being cast forth in that wicked laying out to perish, him they marveled at in the end of the event: not thirsting in like manner to the just.
text.KJV: But for the foolish devices of their wickedness, wherewith being deceived they worshipped serpents void of reason, and vile beasts, thou didst send a multitude of unreasonable beasts upon them for vengeance;
text.NABRE: Second Example: Animals Punish the Egyptians and Benefit the Israelites In return for their senseless, wicked thoughts, which misled them into worshiping dumb serpents and worthless insects, You sent upon them swarms of dumb creatures for vengeance;
text.NRSV: Punishment of the Wicked In return for their foolish and wicked thoughts, which led them astray to worship irrational serpents and worthless animals, you sent upon them a multitude of irrational creatures to punish them,

text.DRB: But for senseless cogitations of their iniquity, for that some erring did worship dumb serpents and superfluous beasts, thou didst send upon them a multitude of dumb beasts for revenge:
text.KJV: That they might know, that wherewithal a man sinneth, by the same also shall he be punished.
text.NABRE: that they might recognize that one is punished by the very things through which one sins.
text.NRSV: so that they might learn that one is punished by the very things by which one sins.

text.DRB: that they might know that by what things a man sinneth, by the same also he is tormented.
text.KJV: For thy Almighty hand, that made the world of matter without form, wanted not means to send among them a multitude of bears or fierce lions,
text.NABRE: Digression on God’s Mercy For not without means was your almighty hand, that had fashioned the universe from formless matter, to send upon them many bears or fierce lions,
text.NRSV: For your all-powerful hand, which created the world out of formless matter, did not lack the means to send upon them a multitude of bears, or bold lions,

text.DRB: For thine omnipotent hand, which made the world of invisible matter, was not unable to send upon them a multitude of bears, or fierce lions,
text.KJV: Or unknown wild beasts, full of rage, newly created, breathing out either a fiery vapour, or filthy scents of scattered smoke, or shooting horrible sparkles out of their eyes:
text.NABRE: Or newly created, wrathful, unknown beasts breathing forth fiery breath, Or pouring out roaring smoke, or flashing terrible sparks from their eyes.
text.NRSV: or newly-created unknown beasts full of rage, or such as breathe out fiery breath, or belch forth a thick pall of smoke, or flash terrible sparks from their eyes;

text.DRB: or unknown beasts full of anger of a new kind; or breathing the vapour of fires, or casting forth the savour of smoke, or shooting horrible sparks from their eyes:
text.KJV: Whereof not only the harm might dispatch them at once, but also the terrible sight utterly destroy them.
text.NABRE: Not only could these attack and completely destroy them; even their frightful appearance itself could slay.
text.NRSV: not only could the harm they did destroy people, but the mere sight of them could kill by fright.

text.DRB: of which not only their hurt was able to destroy them, but also their sight to kill them for fear.
text.KJV: Yea, and without these might they have fallen down with one blast, being persecuted of vengeance, and scattered abroad through the breath of thy power: but thou hast ordered all things in measure and number and weight.
text.NABRE: Even without these, they could have been killed at a single blast, pursued by justice and winnowed by your mighty spirit. But you have disposed all things by measure and number and weight.
text.NRSV: Even apart from these, people could fall at a single breath when pursued by justice and scattered by the breath of your power. But you have arranged all things by measure and number and weight.

text.KJV: For thou canst shew thy great strength at all times when thou wilt; and who may withstand the power of thine arm?
text.NABRE: For great strength is always present with you; who can resist the might of your arm?
text.NRSV: God Is Powerful and Merciful For it is always in your power to show great strength, and who can withstand the might of your arm?
text.DRB: Yea and without these with one spirit they might have been slain suffering persecution of their own facts, and dispersed by the spirit of thy power: but thou hast disposed all things in measure, and number, and weight.

text.KJV: For the whole world before thee is as a little grain of the balance, yea, as a drop of the morning dew that falleth down upon the earth.
text.NABRE: Indeed, before you the whole universe is like a grain from a balance, or a drop of morning dew come down upon the earth.
text.NRSV: Because the whole world before you is like a speck that tips the scales, and like a drop of morning dew that falls on the ground.
text.DRB: For, to be of great force rested always in thee only: & who shall resist the power of thine arm?

text.KJV: But thou hast mercy upon all; for thou canst do all things, and winkest at the sins of men, because they should amend.
text.NABRE: But you have mercy on all, because you can do all things; and you overlook sins for the sake of repentance.
text.NRSV: But you are merciful to all, for you can do all things, and you overlook people’s sins, so that they may repent.
text.DRB: Because as the least weight of the balance, so is the round world before thee, and as a drop of the dew before day, that falleth upon the earth.

text.KJV: For thou lovest all the things that are, and abhorrest nothing which thou hast made: for never wouldest thou have made any thing, if thou hadst hated it.
text.NABRE: For you love all things that are and loathe nothing that you have made; for you would not fashion what you hate.
text.NRSV: For you love all things that exist, and detest none of the things that you have made, for you would not have made anything if you had hated it.
text.DRB: But thou hast mercy on all, because thou canst do all things, and dissemblest the sins of men for repentance.
For thou lovest all things that are, and hatest nothing of those which thou hast made: for thou didst not ordain or make any thing hating it.
text.KJV: And how could any thing have endured, if it had not been thy will? or been preserved, if not called by thee?
text.NABRE: How could a thing remain, unless you willed it; or be preserved, had it not been called forth by you?
text.NRSV: How would anything have endured if you had not willed it? Or how would anything not called forth by you have been preserved?

text.KJV: But thou sparest all: for they are thine, O Lord, thou lover of souls.
text.NABRE: But you spare all things, because they are yours, O Ruler and Lover of souls, for your imperishable spirit is in all things!
text.NRSV: You spare all things, for they are yours, O Lord, you who love the living.
text.DRB: And how could any thing continue, unless thou wouldest? Or be preserved which was not called of thee?
But thou sparest all: because they are thine o Lord, which lovest souls.

text.KJV: For thine incorruptible Spirit is in all things.
text.DRB: Ohow good and sweet is thy spirit o Lord in all!

text.KJV: Therefore chastenest thou them by little and little that offend, and warnest them by putting them in remembrance wherein they have offended, that leaving their wickedness they may believe on thee, O Lord.
text.NRSV: Therefore you correct little by little those who trespass, and you remind and warn them of the things through which they sin, so that they may be freed from wickedness and put their trust in you, O Lord.
text.DRB: And therefore those that err, by parts thou doest chastise: and doest admonish, and speak to them, concerning the things wherein they sin: that leaving naughtiness, they may believe in thee o Lord.
For those old inhabitants of thy holy land, whom thou didst abhor,
text.KJV: For it was thy will to destroy by the hands of our fathers both those old inhabitants of thy holy land,
text.NRSV: The Sins of the Canaanites Those who lived long ago in your holy land

text.DRB: because they did works odious to thee by sorceries, and unjust sacrifices,
text.KJV: Whom thou hatedst for doing most odious works of witchcrafts, and wicked sacrifices;
text.NRSV: you hated for their detestable practices, their works of sorcery and unholy rites,

text.DRB: and the murderers of their own children without mercy, and eaters of men's bowels, and devourers of blood from the midst of thy sacrament,
text.KJV: And also those merciless murderers of children, and devourers of man’s flesh, and the feasts of blood,
text.NRSV: their merciless slaughter of children, and their sacrificial feasting on human flesh and blood. These initiates from the midst of a heathen cult,

text.DRB: and the parents authors of aidless souls, thou wouldst destroy by the hands of our parents,
text.KJV: With their priests out of the midst of their idolatrous crew, and the parents, that killed with their own hands souls destitute of help:
text.NRSV: these parents who murder helpless lives, you willed to destroy by the hands of our ancestors,

text.DRB: that they might receive a peregrination worthy of the children of God, which is a land of all most dear to thee.
text.KJV: That the land, which thou esteemedst above all other, might receive a worthy colony of God’s children.
text.NRSV: so that the land most precious of all to you might receive a worthy colony of the servants of God.

text.DRB: But them also as men thou didst spare, and didst send forerunners of thine host, wasps, that by little and little they might destroy them.
text.KJV: Nevertheless even those thou sparedst as men, and didst send wasps, forerunners of thine host, to destroy them by little and little.
text.NRSV: But even these you spared, since they were but mortals, and sent wasps as forerunners of your army to destroy them little by little,

text.DRB: Not because thou wast unable in battle to subdue the impious to the just, or with cruel beasts, or with a sharp word to destroy them together:
text.KJV: Not that thou wast unable to bring the ungodly under the hand of the righteous in battle, or to destroy them at once with cruel beasts, or with one rough word:
text.NRSV: though you were not unable to give the ungodly into the hands of the righteous in battle, or to destroy them at one blow by dread wild animals or your stern word.

text.KJV: But executing thy judgments upon them by little and little, thou gavest them place of repentance, not being ignorant that they were a naughty generation, and that their malice was bred in them, and that their cogitation would never be changed.
text.NRSV: But judging them little by little you gave them an opportunity to repent, though you were not unaware that their origin was evil and their wickedness inborn, and that their way of thinking would never change.
text.DRB: but judging by parts thou gavest place of repentance, being not ignorant, that the nation of them is wicked, and their malice natural & that their cogitation could not be changed for ever.
For it was a cursed seed from the beginning: neither fearing any, didst thou give pardon to their sins.
text.KJV: For it was a cursed seed from the beginning; neither didst thou for fear of any man give them pardon for those things wherein they sinned.
text.NRSV: For they were an accursed race from the beginning, and it was not through fear of anyone that you left them unpunished for their sins.

text.DRB: For who shall say to thee: what hast thou done? Or who shall stand against thy judgement? Or who in thy sight shall come revenger of the wicked men? Or who shall impute it to thee, if the nations perish, which thou hast made?
text.KJV: For who shall say, What hast thou done? or who shall withstand thy judgment? or who shall accuse thee for the nations that perish, whom thou made? or who shall come to stand against thee, to be revenged for the unrighteous men?
text.NRSV: God Is Sovereign For who will say, ‘What have you done?’ or will resist your judgement? Who will accuse you for the destruction of nations that you made? Or who will come before you to plead as an advocate for the unrighteous?

text.KJV: For neither is there any God but thou that careth for all, to whom thou mightest shew that thy judgment is not unright.
text.NRSV: For neither is there any god besides you, whose care is for all people, to whom you should prove that you have not judged unjustly;
text.DRB: For there is no other God but thou, who hast care of all, that thou mayst shew that thou doest not give judgement unjustly.
Neither king, nor tyrant in thy sight shall inquire of them, whom thou hast destroyed.
text.KJV: Neither shall king or tyrant be able to set his face against thee for any whom thou hast punished.
text.NRSV: nor can any king or monarch confront you about those whom you have punished.

text.KJV: Forsomuch then as thou art righteous thyself, thou orderest all things righteously: thinking it not agreeable with thy power to condemn him that hath not deserved to be punished.
text.NRSV: You are righteous and you rule all things righteously, deeming it alien to your power to condemn anyone who does not deserve to be punished.
text.DRB: For so much then as thou art just, thou doest dispose all things justly: thou also esteemest it disagreeable from thy power to condemn him, who ought not to be punished.

text.KJV: For thy power is the beginning of righteousness, and because thou art the Lord of all, it maketh thee to be gracious unto all.
text.NRSV: For your strength is the source of righteousness, and your sovereignty over all causes you to spare all.
text.DRB: For thy power is the beginning of justice: and for this that thou art Lord of all, thou makest thyself to spare all.
For thou shewest power, which art not thought to be absolute in power, and thou convincest the boldness of them, that know thee not.
text.KJV: For when men will not believe that thou art of a full power, thou shewest thy strength, and among them that know it thou makest their boldness manifest.
text.NRSV: For you show your strength when people doubt the completeness of your power, and you rebuke any insolence among those who know it.

text.DRB: But thou dominatour of power, judgest with tranquility, and with great reverence disposest of us: for it is in thy power when thou wilt, to be able.
text.KJV: But thou, mastering thy power, judgest with equity, and orderest us with great favour: for thou mayest use power when thou wilt.
text.NRSV: Although you are sovereign in strength, you judge with mildness, and with great forbearance you govern us; for you have power to act whenever you choose.

text.DRB: And thou hast taught thy people by such works, that they must be just and gentle, and hast made thy children of good hope: because judging thou givest in sins place of repentance.
text.KJV: But by such works hast thou taught thy people that the just man should be merciful, and hast made thy children to be of a good hope that thou givest repentance for sins.
text.NRSV: God’s Lessons for Israel Through such works you have taught your people that the righteous must be kind, and you have filled your children with good hope, because you give repentance for sins.

text.DRB: For if thou didst punish the enemies of thy servants, and that deserved to die, with so great consideration, giving time and place, whereby they might be changed from their wickedness:
text.KJV: For if thou didst punish the enemies of thy children, and the condemned to death, with such deliberation, giving them time and place, whereby they might be delivered from their malice:
text.NRSV: For if you punished with such great care and indulgence the enemies of your servants and those deserving of death, granting them time and opportunity to give up their wickedness,

text.DRB: with what diligence hast thou judged thy children, to whose parents thou gavest oaths and covenants of good promises?
text.KJV: With how great circumspection didst thou judge thine own sons, unto whose fathers thou hast sworn, and made covenants of good promises?
text.NRSV: with what strictness you have judged your children, to whose ancestors you gave oaths and covenants full of good promises!

text.DRB: Therefore when thou givest us discipline, thou scourgest our enemies very many ways, that judging we may think upon thy goodness: and when we are judged, we may hope for thy mercy.
text.KJV: Therefore, whereas thou dost chasten us, thou scourgest our enemies a thousand times more, to the intent that, when we judge, we should carefully think of thy goodness, and when we ourselves are judged, we should look for mercy.
text.NRSV: So while chastening us you scourge our enemies ten thousand times more, so that, when we judge, we may meditate upon your goodness, and when we are judged, we may expect mercy.

text.DRB: Wherefore to them also, which in their life have lived foolishly & unjustly, thou hast given great torments by the same things, which they did worship.
text.KJV: Wherefore, whereas men have lived dissolutely and unrighteously, thou hast tormented them with their own abominations.
text.NRSV: The Punishment of the Egyptians Therefore those who lived unrighteously, in a life of folly, you tormented through their own abominations.

text.DRB: For they wandered long in the way of error, esteeming for gods those things, that in beasts are superfluous, living after the manner of senseless infants.
text.KJV: For they went astray very far in the ways of error, and held them for gods, which even among the beasts of their enemies were despised, being deceived, as children of no understanding.
text.NRSV: For they went far astray on the paths of error, accepting as gods those animals that even their enemies despised; they were deceived like foolish infants.

text.DRB: For this cause thou hast given judgement on them as on senseless children to be in derision.
text.KJV: Therefore unto them, as to children without the use of reason, thou didst send a judgment to mock them.
text.NRSV: Therefore, as though to children who cannot reason, you sent your judgement to mock them.

text.DRB: But they that were not amended by scorns and reprehensions, have tried the worthy judgment of God.
text.KJV: But they that would not be reformed by that correction, wherein he dallied with them, shall feel a judgment worthy of God.
text.NRSV: But those who have not heeded the warning of mild rebukes will experience the deserved judgement of God.

text.DRB: For in what things they suffering took indignation, by those whom they thought gods, when seeing they were destroyed in them, him, whom in time past they denied that they knew, they acknowledged the true God: for the which cause the end also of their condemnation shall come upon them.
text.KJV: For, look, for what things they grudged, when they were punished, that is, for them whom they thought to be gods; now being punished in them, when they saw it, they acknowledged him to be the true God, whom before they denied to know: and therefore came extreme damnation upon them.
text.NRSV: For when in their suffering they became incensed at those creatures that they had thought to be gods, being punished by means of them, they saw and recognized as the true God the one whom they had before refused to know. Therefore the utmost condemnation came upon them.

text.KJV: Surely vain are all men by nature, who are ignorant of God, and could not out of the good things that are seen know him that is: neither by considering the works did they acknowledge the workmaster;
text.NABRE: Chapter 13 Digression on False Worship A. Nature Worship Foolish by nature were all who were in ignorance of God, and who from the good things seen did not succeed in knowing the one who is, and from studying the works did not discern the artisan;
text.NRSV: The Foolishness of Nature Worship 13 and they were unable from the good things that are seen to know the one who exists, nor did they recognize the artisan while paying heed to his works;
text.DRB: But all men be vain, in whom there is not the knowledge of God: and of these good things which are seen they could not understand him that is, neither attending to the works have they agnised who was the workman:

text.KJV: But deemed either fire, or wind, or the swift air, or the circle of the stars, or the violent water, or the lights of heaven, to be the gods which govern the world.
text.NABRE: Instead either fire, or wind, or the swift air, or the circuit of the stars, or the mighty water, or the luminaries of heaven, the governors of the world, they considered gods.
text.NRSV: but they supposed that either fire or wind or swift air, or the circle of the stars, or turbulent water, or the luminaries of heaven were the gods that rule the world.
text.DRB: but either the fire, or the wind, or the swift air, or a circle of stars, or exceeding much water, or the sun and moon, they thought to be gods, rulers of the world.

text.KJV: With whose beauty if they being delighted took them to be gods; let them know how much better the Lord of them is: for the first author of beauty hath created them.
text.NABRE: Now if out of joy in their beauty they thought them gods, let them know how far more excellent is the Lord than these; for the original source of beauty fashioned them.
text.NRSV: If through delight in the beauty of these things people assumed them to be gods, let them know how much better than these is their Lord, for the author of beauty created them.
text.DRB: With whose beauty if being delighted they thought them gods: let them know how much the Lord of them is more beautiful than they. For the author of beauty made all those things.
Or if they marveled at their virtue, & operations, let them understand by them, that he which made these, is stronger than they:
text.KJV: But if they were astonished at their power and virtue, let them understand by them, how much mightier he is that made them.
text.NABRE: Or if they were struck by their might and energy, let them realize from these things how much more powerful is the one who made them.
text.NRSV: And if people were amazed at their power and working, let them perceive from them how much more powerful is the one who formed them.

text.KJV: For by the greatness and beauty of the creatures proportionably the maker of them is seen.
text.NABRE: For from the greatness and the beauty of created things their original author, by analogy, is seen.
text.NRSV: For from the greatness and beauty of created things comes a corresponding perception of their Creator.
text.DRB: for by the greatness of the beauty, and of the creature, the Creator of them may be seen, to be known thereby.
But notwithstanding there is yet in these less complaint. For they also perhaps err, seeking God, and desirous to find him.
text.KJV: But yet for this they are the less to be blamed: for they peradventure err, seeking God, and desirous to find him.
text.NABRE: But yet, for these the blame is less; For they have gone astray perhaps, though they seek God and wish to find him.
text.NRSV: Yet these people are little to be blamed, for perhaps they go astray while seeking God and desiring to find him.

text.DRB: For whereas they converse in his works, they inquire: & they are persuaded that the things be good which are seen.
text.KJV: For being conversant in his works they search him diligently, and believe their sight: because the things are beautiful that are seen.
text.NABRE: For they search busily among his works, but are distracted by what they see, because the things seen are fair.
text.NRSV: For while they live among his works, they keep searching, and they trust in what they see, because the things that are seen are beautiful.

text.DRB: But again neither ought these to be pardoned.
text.KJV: Howbeit neither are they to be pardoned.
text.NABRE: But again, not even these are pardonable.
text.NRSV: Yet again, not even they are to be excused;

text.DRB: For if they could know so much, that they were able to estimate the world: how did they not more easily find the Lord thereof?
text.KJV: For if they were able to know so much, that they could aim at the world; how did they not sooner find out the Lord thereof?
text.NABRE: For if they so far succeeded in knowledge that they could speculate about the world, how did they not more quickly find its Lord?
text.NRSV: for if they had the power to know so much that they could investigate the world, how did they fail to find sooner the Lord of these things?

text.KJV: But miserable are they, and in dead things is their hope, who call them gods, which are the works of men’s hands, gold and silver, to shew art in, and resemblances of beasts, or a stone good for nothing, the work of an ancient hand.
text.NABRE: B. Idolatry But wretched are they, and in dead things are their hopes, who termed gods things made by human hands: Gold and silver, the product of art, and images of beasts, or useless stone, the work of an ancient hand.
text.NRSV: The Foolishness of Idolatry But miserable, with their hopes set on dead things, are those who give the name ‘gods’ to the works of human hands, gold and silver fashioned with skill, and likenesses of animals, or a useless stone, the work of an ancient hand.
text.DRB: But they are unhappy, & their hope is among the dead, who have called the works of men's hands gods, gold & silver, the invention of art, and the similitudes of beasts, or an unprofitable stone the work of an old hand.

Or if an artificer a carpenter, cut straight timber out of the wood, & pare off all the bark thereof cunningly, and using his art diligently frameth a vessel profitable for the common use of this life,
text.KJV: Now a carpenter that felleth timber, after he hath sawn down a tree meet for the purpose, and taken off all the bark skilfully round about, and hath wrought it handsomely, and made a vessel thereof fit for the service of man’s life;
text.NABRE: The Carpenter and Wooden Idols A carpenter may cut down a suitable tree and skillfully scrape off all its bark, And deftly plying his art produce something fit for daily use,
text.NRSV: A skilled woodcutter may saw down a tree easy to handle and skilfully strip off all its bark, and then with pleasing workmanship make a useful vessel that serves life’s needs,

text.DRB: and useth the chips of that work to dress his meat:
text.KJV: And after spending the refuse of his work to dress his meat, hath filled himself;
text.NABRE: And use the scraps from his handiwork in preparing his food, and have his fill;
text.NRSV: and burn the cast-off pieces of his work to prepare his food, and eat his fill.

text.DRB: and maketh that which is left thereof, which is for no uses, but being a crooked piece of wood, and full of knobs, carveth it diligently in the hollowness thereof, and by the skill of his art fashioneth it, and maketh it like to the image of a man,
text.KJV: And taking the very refuse among those which served to no use, being a crooked piece of wood, and full of knots, hath carved it diligently, when he had nothing else to do, and formed it by the skill of his understanding, and fashioned it to the image of a man;
text.NABRE: Then the good-for-nothing refuse from these remnants, crooked wood grown full of knots, he takes and carves to occupy his spare time. This wood he models with mindless skill, and patterns it on the image of a human being
text.NRSV: But a cast-off piece from among them, useful for nothing, a stick crooked and full of knots, he takes and carves with care in his leisure, and shapes it with skill gained in idleness; he forms it in the likeness of a human being,

text.DRB: or compareth it to some beast, straking it over with red, and with painting making the colour thereof ruddy, and layeth a colour over every spot that is in it:
text.KJV: Or made it like some vile beast, laying it over with vermilion, and with paint colouring it red, and covering every spot therein;
text.NABRE: or makes it resemble some worthless beast. When he has daubed it with red and crimsoned its surface with red stain, and daubed over every blemish in it,
text.NRSV: or makes it like some worthless animal, giving it a coat of red paint and colouring its surface red and covering every blemish in it with paint;

text.DRB: and maketh a worthy habitation for it, and setting it in a wall, and fastening it with iron
text.KJV: And when he had made a convenient room for it, set it in a wall, and made it fast with iron:
text.NABRE: He makes a fitting shrine for it and puts it on the wall, fastening it with a nail.
text.NRSV: then he makes a suitable niche for it, and sets it in the wall, and fastens it there with iron.

text.DRB: lest perhaps it fall, providing for it, knowing that it cannot help itself: for it is an image, and it needeth help.
text.KJV: For he provided for it that it might not fall, knowing that it was unable to help itself; for it is an image, and hath need of help:
text.NABRE: Thus he provides for it lest it fall down, knowing that it cannot help itself; for, truly, it is an image and needs help.
text.NRSV: He takes thought for it, so that it may not fall, because he knows that it cannot help itself, for it is only an image and has need of help.

text.DRB: And concerning his substance, & his children, & for marriage, making a vow he seeketh to it. He is not ashamed to speak with him, that is without soul:
text.KJV: Then maketh he prayer for his goods, for his wife and children, and is not ashamed to speak to that which hath no life.
text.NABRE: But when he prays about his goods or marriage or children, he is not ashamed to address the thing without a soul. For vigor he invokes the powerless;
text.NRSV: When he prays about possessions and his marriage and children, he is not ashamed to address a lifeless thing.

text.DRB: and for health certes he beseecheth the weak, and for life asketh the dead, and for help invocateth him that is unprofitable:
text.KJV: For health he calleth upon that which is weak: for life prayeth to that which is dead; for aid humbly beseecheth that which hath least means to help: and for a good journey he asketh of that which cannot set a foot forward:
text.NABRE: for life he entreats the dead; For aid he beseeches the wholly incompetent; for travel, something that cannot even walk;
text.NRSV: For health he appeals to a thing that is weak; for life he prays to a thing that is dead; for aid he entreats a thing that is utterly inexperienced; for a prosperous journey, a thing that cannot take a step;

text.DRB: and for a journey asketh him, that cannot walk: and for getting, and for working, and for the event of all things he asketh him, that in all is unprofitable.
text.KJV: And for gaining and getting, and for good success of his hands, asketh ability to do of him, that is most unable to do any thing.
text.NABRE: For profit in business and success with his hands he asks power of a thing with hands utterly powerless.
text.NRSV: for money-making and work and success with his hands he asks strength of a thing whose hands have no strength.

text.DRB: Again another thinking to sail, and beginning to make a journey through the fierce waves, invocateth wood more frail than the wood that carrieth him.
text.KJV: Again, one preparing himself to sail, and about to pass through the raging waves, calleth upon a piece of wood more rotten than the vessel that carrieth him.
text.NABRE: Chapter 14 Again, one preparing for a voyage and about to traverse the wild waves cries out to wood more unsound than the boat that bears him.
text.NRSV: Folly of a Navigator Praying to an Idol 14 calls upon a piece of wood more fragile than the ship that carries him.

text.DRB: For covetousness of getting invented it, and the craftsman by his wisdom framed it.
text.KJV: For verily desire of gain devised that, and the workman built it by his skill.
text.NABRE: For the urge for profits devised this latter, and Wisdom the artisan produced it.
text.NRSV: For it was desire for gain that planned that vessel, and wisdom was the artisan who built it;

text.DRB: But thy providence, o Father, doth govern: because thou hast given a way even in the sea, and among the waves a most sure path,
text.KJV: But thy providence, O Father, governeth it: for thou hast made a way in the sea, and a safe path in the waves;
text.NABRE: But your providence, O Father! guides it, for you have furnished even in the sea a road, and through the waves a steady path,
text.NRSV: but it is your providence, O Father, that steers its course, because you have given it a path in the sea, and a safe way through the waves,

text.DRB: shewing that thou art able to save out of all things, yea if a man go to the sea without art.
text.KJV: Shewing that thou canst save from all danger: yea, though a man went to sea without art.
text.NABRE: Showing that you can save from any danger, so that even one without skill may embark.
text.NRSV: showing that you can save from every danger, so that even a person who lacks skill may put to sea.

text.DRB: But that thy works might not be void of wisdom: for this cause also men commit their lives even to a little wood, and passing over the sea are delivered by ship.
text.KJV: Nevertheless thou wouldest not that the works of thy wisdom should be idle, and therefore do men commit their lives to a small piece of wood, and passing the rough sea in a weak vessel are saved.
text.NABRE: But you will that the products of your Wisdom be not idle; therefore people trust their lives even to most frail wood, and were safe crossing the waves on a raft.
text.NRSV: It is your will that works of your wisdom should not be without effect; therefore people trust their lives even to the smallest piece of wood, and passing through the billows on a raft they come safely to land.

text.DRB: But from the beginning also when the proud giants perished, the hope of the world flying to a ship, rendered to the world seed of nativity, which was governed by thy hand.
text.KJV: For in the old time also, when the proud giants perished, the hope of the world governed by thy hand escaped in a weak vessel, and left to all ages a seed of generation.
text.NABRE: For of old, when the proud giants were being destroyed, the hope of the universe, who took refuge on a raft, left to the world a future for the human family, under the guidance of your hand.
text.NRSV: For even in the beginning, when arrogant giants were perishing, the hope of the world took refuge on a raft, and guided by your hand left to the world the seed of a new generation.

text.DRB: For blessed is the wood, by the which justice is made.
text.KJV: For blessed is the wood whereby righteousness cometh.
text.NABRE: For blest is the wood through which righteousness comes about;
text.NRSV: For blessed is the wood by which righteousness comes.

text.DRB: But the idol that is made by hands, cursed is both it, and he that made it: because he indeed wrought it: and the same being frail, was called God.
text.KJV: But that which is made with hands is cursed, as well it, as he that made it: he, because he made it; and it, because, being corruptible, it was called god.
text.NABRE: but the handmade idol is accursed, and its maker as well: he for having produced it, and the corruptible thing, because it was termed a god.
text.NRSV: But the idol made with hands is accursed, and so is the one who made it— he for having made it, and the perishable thing because it was named a god.

text.DRB: But to God the impious and his impiety are odious alike.
text.KJV: For the ungodly and his ungodliness are both alike hateful unto God.
text.NABRE: Equally odious to God are the evildoer and the evil deed;
text.NRSV: For equally hateful to God are the ungodly and their ungodliness;

text.DRB: For that which is made, with him that made it, shall suffer torments.
text.KJV: For that which is made shall be punished together with him that made it.
text.NABRE: and the thing made will be punished with its maker.
text.NRSV: for what was done will be punished together with the one who did it.

text.DRB: For this cause also in the idol of the nations there shall be no respect: because the creatures of God were made to hatred, and for tentation to the souls of men, and for a snare to the feet of the unwise.
text.KJV: Therefore even upon the idols of the Gentiles shall there be a visitation: because in the creature of God they are become an abomination, and stumblingblocks to the souls of men, and a snare to the feet of the unwise.
text.NABRE: Therefore upon even the idols of the nations shall a judgment come, since they became abominable among God’s works, Snares for human souls and a trap for the feet of the senseless.
text.NRSV: Therefore there will be a visitation also upon the heathen idols, because, though part of what God created, they became an abomination, snares for human souls and a trap for the feet of the foolish.

text.KJV: For the devising of idols was the beginning of spiritual fornication, and the invention of them the corruption of life.
text.NABRE: The Origin and Evils of Idolatry For the source of wantonness is the devising of idols; and their invention, a corruption of life.
text.NRSV: The Origin and Evils of Idolatry For the idea of making idols was the beginning of fornication, and the invention of them was the corruption of life;
text.DRB: For the beginning of fornication is the devising of idols: and the inventing of them is the corruption of life.

text.KJV: For neither were they from the beginning, neither shall they be for ever.
text.NABRE: For in the beginning they were not, nor can they ever continue;
text.NRSV: for they did not exist from the beginning, nor will they last for ever.
text.DRB: For neither were they from the beginning, neither shall they be for ever.
For this vanity of men came into the world: and therefore there is found a short end of them.
text.KJV: For by the vain glory of men they entered into the world, and therefore shall they come shortly to an end.
text.NABRE: for from human emptiness they came into the world, and therefore a sudden end is devised for them.
text.NRSV: For through human vanity they entered the world, and therefore their speedy end has been planned.

text.DRB: For the father being sorrowful with bitter mourning, made unto himself the image of his son quickly taken away: and him, that then was a dead man, now he began to worship as God, and appointed holy things and sacrifices among his servants.
text.KJV: For a father afflicted with untimely mourning, when he hath made an image of his child soon taken away, now honoured him as a god, which was then a dead man, and delivered to those that were under him ceremonies and sacrifices.
text.NABRE: For a father, afflicted with untimely mourning, made an image of the child so quickly taken from him, And now honored as a god what once was dead and handed down to his household mysteries and sacrifices.
text.NRSV: For a father, consumed with grief at an untimely bereavement, made an image of his child, who had been suddenly taken from him; he now honoured as a god what was once a dead human being, and handed on to his dependants secret rites and initiations.

text.DRB: Afterward by succession of time, the wicked custom prevailing, this error was kept as a law, and things graven were worshipped by the commandment of Tyrants.
text.KJV: Thus in process of time an ungodly custom grown strong was kept as a law, and graven images were worshipped by the commandments of kings.
text.NABRE: Then, in the course of time, the impious practice gained strength and was observed as law, and graven things were worshiped by royal decrees.
text.NRSV: Then the ungodly custom, grown strong with time, was kept as a law, and at the command of monarchs carved images were worshipped.

text.DRB: And those, whom openly men could not honour, for that they were far off, their figure being brought from a far, they made an evident image of the king, whom they would honour: that by their carefulness they might honour as present, him that was absent.
