text.KJV: Whom men could not honour in presence, because they dwelt far off, they took the counterfeit of his visage from far, and made an express image of a king whom they honoured, to the end that by this their forwardness they might flatter him that was absent, as if he were present.
text.NABRE: People who lived so far away that they could not honor him in his presence copied the appearance of the distant king And made a public image of him they wished to honor, out of zeal to flatter the absent one as though present.
text.NRSV: When people could not honour monarchs in their presence, since they lived at a distance, they imagined their appearance far away, and made a visible image of the king whom they honoured, so that by their zeal they might flatter the absent one as though present.

text.DRB: And to the worshipping of these, the excellent diligence also of the artificer, holp them forward, that were ignorant.
text.KJV: Also the singular diligence of the artificer did help to set forward the ignorant to more superstition.
text.NABRE: And to promote this observance among those to whom it was strange, the artisan’s ambition provided a stimulus.
text.NRSV: Then the ambition of the artisan impelled even those who did not know the king to intensify their worship.

text.DRB: For he willing to please him that entertained him, laboured by his art to fashion the similitude in better sort.
text.KJV: For he, peradventure willing to please one in authority, forced all his skill to make the resemblance of the best fashion.
text.NABRE: For he, perhaps in his determination to please the ruler, labored over the likeness to the best of his skill;
text.NRSV: For he, perhaps wishing to please his ruler, skilfully forced the likeness to take more beautiful form,

text.DRB: And the multitude of men carried away by the beauty of the work, him that a little before had been honoured as a man, now they esteemed for a God.
text.KJV: And so the multitude, allured by the grace of the work, took him now for a god, which a little before was but honoured.
text.NABRE: And the masses, drawn by the charm of the workmanship, soon took as an object of worship the one who shortly before was honored as a human being.
text.NRSV: and the multitude, attracted by the charm of his work, now regarded as an object of worship the one whom shortly before they had honoured as a human being.

text.DRB: And this was the deceiving of man's life: because men serving either affection, or kings, gave the name that is not communicable, to stones and wood.
text.KJV: And this was an occasion to deceive the world: for men, serving either calamity or tyranny, did ascribe unto stones and stocks the incommunicable name.
text.NABRE: And this became a snare for the world, that people enslaved to either grief or tyranny conferred the incommunicable Name on stones and wood.
text.NRSV: And this became a hidden trap for humankind, because people, in bondage to misfortune or to royal authority, bestowed on objects of stone or wood the name that ought not to be shared.

text.DRB: And it was not sufficient that they erred about the knowledge of God, but also living in a great battle of ignorance, so many and so great evils they call peace.
text.KJV: Moreover this was not enough for them, that they erred in the knowledge of God; but whereas they lived in the great war of ignorance, those so great plagues called they peace.
text.NABRE: Then it was not enough for them to err in their knowledge of God; but even though they live in a great war resulting from ignorance, they call such evils peace.
text.NRSV: Then it was not enough for them to err about the knowledge of God, but though living in great strife due to ignorance, they call such great evils peace.

text.DRB: For either sacrificing their children, or making obscure sacrifices, or having watches full of madness,
text.KJV: For whilst they slew their children in sacrifices, or used secret ceremonies, or made revellings of strange rites;
text.NABRE: For while they practice either child sacrifices or occult mysteries, or frenzied carousing in exotic rites,
text.NRSV: For whether they kill children in their initiations, or celebrate secret mysteries, or hold frenzied revels with strange customs,

text.DRB: they now neither keep life, nor marriage clean, but one killeth another by envy, or playing the adulterer maketh him sorrowful:
text.KJV: They kept neither lives nor marriages any longer undefiled: but either one slew another traitorously, or grieved him by adultery.
text.NABRE: They no longer respect either lives or purity of marriage; but they either waylay and kill each other, or aggrieve each other by adultery.
text.NRSV: they no longer keep either their lives or their marriages pure, but they either treacherously kill one another, or grieve one another by adultery,

text.DRB: and all things are mingled together, blood, manslaughter, theft and fiction, corruption and infidelity, trouble and perjury, disquieting of the good,
text.KJV: So that there reigned in all men without exception blood, manslaughter, theft, and dissimulation, corruption, unfaithfulness, tumults, perjury,
text.NABRE: And all is confusion—blood and murder, theft and guile, corruption, faithlessness, turmoil, perjury,
text.NRSV: and all is a raging riot of blood and murder, theft and deceit, corruption, faithlessness, tumult, perjury,

text.DRB: forgetfulness of God, inquination of souls, immutation of nativity, inconstancy of marriage, disorder of adultery, and unchasteness.
text.KJV: Disquieting of good men, forgetfulness of good turns, defiling of souls, changing of kind, disorder in marriages, adultery, and shameless uncleanness.
text.NABRE: Disturbance of good people, neglect of gratitude, besmirching of souls, unnatural lust, disorder in marriage, adultery and shamelessness.
text.NRSV: confusion over what is good, forgetfulness of favours, defiling of souls, sexual perversion, disorder in marriages, adultery, and debauchery.

text.KJV: For the worshipping of idols not to be named is the beginning, the cause, and the end, of all evil.
text.NABRE: For the worship of infamous idols is the reason and source and extreme of all evil.
text.NRSV: For the worship of idols not to be named is the beginning and cause and end of every evil.
text.DRB: For the worship of idols not to be named, is the cause of all evil, and the beginning and end.
For either when they rejoice, they are mad: or certes prophesy false things, or live unjustly, or quickly forswear themselves.
text.KJV: For either they are mad when they be merry, or prophesy lies, or live unjustly, or else lightly forswear themselves.
text.NABRE: For they either go mad with enjoyment, or prophesy lies, or live lawlessly or lightly perjure themselves.
text.NRSV: For their worshippers either rave in exultation, or prophesy lies, or live unrighteously, or readily commit perjury;

text.DRB: For whilst they trust in idols, which are without soul, swearing amiss they hope not to be hurt.
text.KJV: For insomuch as their trust is in idols, which have no life; though they swear falsely, yet they look not to be hurt.
text.NABRE: For as their trust is in lifeless idols, they expect no harm when they have sworn falsely.
text.NRSV: for because they trust in lifeless idols they swear wicked oaths and expect to suffer no harm.

text.DRB: Two evil things therefore shall happen to them worthily, because they have thought evil of God, attending to idols, and have sworn unjustly, in guile contemning justice.
text.KJV: Howbeit for both causes shall they be justly punished: both because they thought not well of God, giving heed unto idols, and also unjustly swore in deceit, despising holiness.
text.NABRE: But on both counts justice shall overtake them: because they thought perversely of God by devoting themselves to idols, and because they deliberately swore false oaths, despising piety.
text.NRSV: But just penalties will overtake them on two counts: because they thought wrongly about God in devoting themselves to idols, and because in deceit they swore unrighteously through contempt for holiness.

text.DRB: For it is not the power of them that are sworn by, but the punishment of them that sin goeth always through the transgression of the unjust.
text.KJV: For it is not the power of them by whom they swear: but it is the just vengeance of sinners, that punisheth always the offence of the ungodly.
text.NABRE: For it is not the might of those by whom they swear, but the just retribution of sinners, that ever follows upon the transgression of the wicked.
text.NRSV: For it is not the power of the things by which people swear, but the just penalty for those who sin, that always pursues the transgression of the unrighteous.

text.KJV: But thou, O God, art gracious and true, longsuffering, and in mercy ordering all things,
text.NABRE: Chapter 15 But you, our God, are good and true, slow to anger, and governing all with mercy.
text.NRSV: Benefits of Worshipping the True God 15 patient, and ruling all things in mercy.
text.DRB: But o thou our God art sweet and true, patient, and disposing all things in mercy.

text.KJV: For if we sin, we are thine, knowing thy power: but we will not sin, knowing that we are counted thine.
text.NABRE: For even if we sin, we are yours, and know your might; but we will not sin, knowing that we belong to you.
text.NRSV: For even if we sin we are yours, knowing your power; but we will not sin, because we know that you acknowledge us as yours.
text.DRB: For if we sin, we are thine, knowing thy greatness: and if we sin not, we know that we are counted with thee.

text.KJV: For to know thee is perfect righteousness: yea, to know thy power is the root of immortality.
text.NABRE: For to know you well is complete righteousness, and to know your might is the root of immortality.
text.NRSV: For to know you is complete righteousness, and to know your power is the root of immortality.
text.DRB: For to know thee, is absolute justice: and to know justice, and thy power, is the root of immortality.
For men's invention of evil art hath not brought us into error, nor the shadow of a picture being a labour without fruit, a shape graven by diverse colours,
text.KJV: For neither did the mischievous invention of men deceive us, nor an image spotted with divers colours, the painter’s fruitless labour;
text.NABRE: For the evil creation of human fancy did not deceive us, nor the fruitless labor of painters, A form smeared with varied colors,
text.NRSV: For neither has the evil intent of human art misled us, nor the fruitless toil of painters, a figure stained with varied colours,

text.DRB: the sight whereof giveth concupiscence to the senseless, and he loveth the shape without life of a dead image.
text.KJV: The sight whereof enticeth fools to lust after it, and so they desire the form of a dead image, that hath no breath.
text.NABRE: the sight of which arouses yearning in a fool, till he longs for the inanimate form of a dead image.
text.NRSV: whose appearance arouses yearning in fools, so that they desire the lifeless form of a dead image.

text.DRB: The lovers of evils are worthy to have their hope in such things, both they that make them, and that love, and that worship them.
text.KJV: Both they that make them, they that desire them, and they that worship them, are lovers of evil things, and are worthy to have such things to trust upon.
text.NABRE: Lovers of evil things, and worthy of such hopes are they who make them and long for them and worship them.
text.NRSV: Lovers of evil things and fit for such objects of hope are those who either make or desire or worship them.

text.DRB: Yea and the potter pressing soft earth, with labour fashioneth every vessel to our uses, and of the same clay maketh the vessels that are clean to use, and in like manner them, that are contrary to these: but what the use of these vessels is, the potter is judge.
text.KJV: For the potter, tempering soft earth, fashioneth every vessel with much labour for our service: yea, of the same clay he maketh both the vessels that serve for clean uses, and likewise also all such as serve to the contrary: but what is the use of either sort, the potter himself is the judge.
text.NABRE: The Potter’s Clay Idols For the potter, laboriously working the soft earth, molds for our service each single article: He fashions out of the same clay both the vessels that serve for clean purposes and their opposites, all alike; As to what shall be the use of each vessel of either class the worker in clay is the judge.
text.NRSV: The Foolishness of Worshipping Clay Idols A potter kneads the soft earth and laboriously moulds each vessel for our service, fashioning out of the same clay both the vessels that serve clean uses and those for contrary uses, making all alike; but which shall be the use of each of them the worker in clay decides.

text.DRB: And with vain labour he fashioneth a god of the same clay: he which a little before was made of earth, and a little after returneth back whence he was taken, being exacted the debt of the life which he had.
text.KJV: And employing his labours lewdly, he maketh a vain god of the same clay, even he which a little before was made of earth himself, and within a little while after returneth to the same, out when his life which was lent him shall be demanded.
text.NABRE: With misspent toil he molds a meaningless god from the selfsame clay, though he himself shortly before was made from the earth, And is soon to go whence he was taken, when the life that was lent him is demanded back.
text.NRSV: With misspent toil, these workers form a futile god from the same clay— these mortals who were made of earth a short time before and after a little while go to the earth from which all mortals are taken, when the time comes to return the souls that were borrowed.

text.DRB: But his care is, not because he shall labour, nor because he hath a short life, but he contendeth with gold-smiths and silver-smiths: yea and he imitateth the copper-smiths, and counteth it a glory, because he maketh vain things.
text.KJV: Notwithstanding his care is, not that he shall have much labour, nor that his life is short: but striveth to excel goldsmiths and silversmiths, and endeavoureth to do like the workers in brass, and counteth it his glory to make counterfeit things.
text.NABRE: But his concern is not that he is to die nor that his span of life is brief; Rather, he vies with goldsmiths and silversmiths and emulates molders of bronze, and takes pride in fashioning counterfeits.
text.NRSV: But the workers are not concerned that mortals are destined to die or that their life is brief, but they compete with workers in gold and silver, and imitate workers in copper; and they count it a glorious thing to mould counterfeit gods.

text.DRB: For his heart is ashes, and his hope vain earth, and his life viler than clay:
text.KJV: His heart is ashes, his hope is more vile than earth, and his life of less value than clay:
text.NABRE: Ashes his heart is! more worthless than earth is his hope, more ignoble than clay his life;
text.NRSV: Their heart is ashes, their hope is cheaper than dirt, and their lives are of less worth than clay,

text.DRB: because he was ignorant who made him, and who inspired into him the soul which worketh, & who breathed into him the vital spirit.
text.KJV: Forasmuch as he knew not his Maker, and him that inspired into him an active soul, and breathed in a living spirit.
text.NABRE: Because he knew not the one who fashioned him, and breathed into him a quickening soul, and infused a vital spirit.
text.NRSV: because they failed to know the one who formed them and inspired them with active souls and breathed a living spirit into them.

text.DRB: Yea and they esteemed our life to be a pastime, and the conversation of life made for a gain, & that we must get every way even of evil.
text.KJV: But they counted our life a pastime, and our time here a market for gain: for, say they, we must be getting every way, though it be by evil means.
text.NABRE: Instead, he esteemed our life a mere game, and our span of life a holiday for gain; “For one must,” says he, “make a profit in every way, be it even from evil.”
text.NRSV: But they considered our existence an idle game, and life a festival held for profit, for they say one must get money however one can, even by base means.

text.DRB: For he knoweth that he offendeth above all men, which of the matter of earth fashioneth frail vessels, and sculptiles.
text.KJV: For this man, that of earthly matter maketh brittle vessels and graven images, knoweth himself to offend above all others.
text.NABRE: For more than anyone else he knows that he is sinning, when out of earthen stuff he creates fragile vessels and idols alike.
text.NRSV: For these people, more than all others, know that they sin when they make from earthy matter fragile vessels and carved images.

text.DRB: For all the unwise and unhappy above measure of the soul, proud are the enemies of thy people, and rule over them:
text.KJV: And all the enemies of thy people, that hold them in subjection, are most foolish, and are more miserable than very babes.
text.NABRE: But most stupid of all and worse than senseless in mind, are the enemies of your people who enslaved them.
text.NRSV: But most foolish, and more miserable than an infant, are all the enemies who oppressed your people.

text.DRB: because they have esteemed all the idols of the nations for gods, which neither have use of eyes to see, nor nostrils to take breath, nor ears to hear, nor fingers of the hands to handle, yea and their feet are slow to walk.
text.KJV: For they counted all the idols of the heathen to be gods: which neither have the use of eyes to see, nor noses to draw breath, nor ears to hear, nor fingers of hands to handle; and as for their feet, they are slow to go.
text.NABRE: For they esteemed all the idols of the nations as gods, which cannot use their eyes to see, nor nostrils to breathe the air, Nor ears to hear, nor fingers on their hands for feeling; even their feet are useless to walk with.
text.NRSV: For they thought that all their heathen idols were gods, though these have neither the use of their eyes to see with, nor nostrils with which to draw breath, nor ears with which to hear, nor fingers to feel with, and their feet are of no use for walking.

text.DRB: For a man made them: and he that borrowed breath, the same fashioned them. For no man can make God like to himself.
text.KJV: For man made them, and he that borrowed his own spirit fashioned them: but no man can make a god like unto himself.
text.NABRE: For it was a mere human being who made them; one living on borrowed breath who fashioned them. For no one is able to fashion a god like himself;
text.NRSV: For a human being made them, and one whose spirit is borrowed formed them; for none can form gods that are like themselves.

text.DRB: For whereas himself is mortal, he maketh a dead thing with his wicked hands. For he is better than they, whom he worshippeth, because he indeed lived, though he were mortal, but they never.
text.KJV: For being mortal, he worketh a dead thing with wicked hands: for he himself is better than the things which he worshippeth: whereas he lived once, but they never.
text.NABRE: he is mortal, and what he makes with lawless hands is dead. For he is better than the things he worships; he at least lives, but never his idols.
text.NRSV: People are mortal, and what they make with lawless hands is dead; for they are better than the objects they worship, since they have life, but the idols never had.

text.DRB: But they worship also most miserable beasts: for the senseless things compared to these, are worse than they.
text.KJV: Yea, they worshipped those beasts also that are most hateful: for being compared together, some are worse than others.
text.NABRE: Second Example Resumed Besides, they worship the most loathsome beasts— as regards stupidity, these are worse than the rest,
text.NRSV: Serpents in the Desert Moreover, they worship even the most hateful animals, which are worse than all others when judged by their lack of intelligence;

text.DRB: Yea neither by sight can any man see good of these beasts, But they have fled from the praise of God, and from his blessing.
text.KJV: Neither are they beautiful, so much as to be desired in respect of beasts: but they went without the praise of God and his blessing.
text.NABRE: For beasts are neither good-looking nor desirable; they have escaped both the approval of God and his blessing.
text.NRSV: and even as animals they are not so beautiful in appearance that one would desire them, but they have escaped both the praise of God and his blessing.

text.DRB: For these things, and by the like to these, they have worthily suffered torments, and were destroyed by a multitude of beasts.
text.KJV: Therefore by the like were they punished worthily, and by the multitude of beasts tormented.
text.NABRE: Chapter 16 Therefore they were fittingly punished by similar creatures, and were tormented by a swarm of insects.
text.NRSV: 16 and were tormented by a multitude of animals.

text.DRB: For the which torments thou didst well dispose of thy people, to whom thou gavest the desire of their delectation, a new taste, preparing them the quail for meat:
text.KJV: Instead of which punishment, dealing graciously with thine own people, thou preparedst for them meat of a strange taste, even quails to stir up their appetite:
text.NABRE: Instead of this punishment, you benefited your people with a novel dish, the delight they craved, by providing quail for their food,
text.NRSV: Instead of this punishment you showed kindness to your people, and you prepared quails to eat, a delicacy to satisfy the desire of appetite;

text.DRB: that they indeed coveting meat, because of those things which were shewed and sent them, might be turned away even from necessary concupiscence. But they in short time being made needy, tasted a new meat.
text.KJV: To the end that they, desiring food, might for the ugly sight of the beasts sent among them lothe even that, which they must needs desire; but these, suffering penury for a short space, might be made partakers of a strange taste.
text.NABRE: So that those others, when they desired food, should lose their appetite even for necessities, since the creatures sent to plague them were so loathsome, While these, after a brief period of privation, partook of a novel dish.
text.NRSV: in order that those people, when they desired food, might lose the least remnant of appetite because of the odious creatures sent to them, while your people, after suffering want a short time, might partake of delicacies.

text.DRB: For it behoved that without excuse destruction should come upon them exercising tyranny: but to these only to shew how their enemies were destroyed.
text.KJV: For it was requisite, that upon them exercising tyranny should come penury, which they could not avoid: but to these it should only be shewed how their enemies were tormented.
text.NABRE: For inexorable want had to come upon those oppressors; but these needed only to be shown how their enemies were being tormented.
text.NRSV: For it was necessary that upon those oppressors inescapable want should come, while to these others it was merely shown how their enemies were being tormented.

text.DRB: For when the cruel wrath of beasts came upon them, they were destroyed with the bitings of perverse serpents.
text.KJV: For when the horrible fierceness of beasts came upon these, and they perished with the stings of crooked serpents, thy wrath endured not for ever:
text.NABRE: For when the dire venom of beasts came upon them and they were dying from the bite of crooked serpents, your anger endured not to the end.
text.NRSV: For when the terrible rage of wild animals came upon your people and they were being destroyed by the bites of writhing serpents, your wrath did not continue to the end;

text.DRB: Howbeit thy wrath endured not for ever, but for chastisement they were troubled a short time, having a sign of salvation for the remembrance of the commandment of thy law.
text.KJV: But they were troubled for a small season, that they might be admonished, having a sign of salvation, to put them in remembrance of the commandment of thy law.
text.NABRE: But as a warning, for a short time they were terrorized, though they had a sign of salvation, to remind them of the precept of your law.
text.NRSV: they were troubled for a little while as a warning, and received a symbol of deliverance to remind them of your law’s command.

text.KJV: For he that turned himself toward it was not saved by the thing that he saw, but by thee, that art the Saviour of all.
text.NABRE: For the one who turned toward it was saved, not by what was seen, but by you, the savior of all.
text.NRSV: For the one who turned towards it was saved, not by the thing that was beheld, but by you, the Saviour of all.
text.DRB: For he that turned to it, was not healed by that which he saw, but by thee the Saviour of all:
and in this thou didst shew to our enemies, that thou art he which deliverest from all evil.
text.KJV: And in this thou madest thine enemies confess, that it is thou who deliverest from all evil:
text.NABRE: By this also you convinced our foes that you are the one who delivers from all evil.
text.NRSV: And by this also you convinced our enemies that it is you who deliver from every evil.

text.DRB: For the bitings of locusts and flies killed them, and their was found no remedy for their life: because they were worthy to be destroyed by such things.
text.KJV: For them the bitings of grasshoppers and flies killed, neither was there found any remedy for their life: for they were worthy to be punished by such.
text.NABRE: For the bites of locusts and of flies slew them, and no remedy was found to save their lives because they deserved to be punished by such means;
text.NRSV: For they were killed by the bites of locusts and flies, and no healing was found for them, because they deserved to be punished by such things.

text.DRB: But neither the teeth of venomous dragons overcame thy children: for thy mercy coming healed them.
text.KJV: But thy sons not the very teeth of venomous dragons overcame: for thy mercy was ever by them, and healed them.
text.NABRE: But not even the fangs of poisonous reptiles overcame your children, for your mercy came forth and healed them.
text.NRSV: But your children were not conquered even by the fangs of venomous serpents, for your mercy came to their help and healed them.

text.DRB: For in memory of thy words they were examined, & were quickly saved, lest falling into deep oblivion they might not use thy help.
text.KJV: For they were pricked, that they should remember thy words; and were quickly saved, that not falling into deep forgetfulness, they might be continually mindful of thy goodness.
text.NABRE: For as a reminder of your injunctions, they were stung, and swiftly they were saved, Lest they should fall into deep forgetfulness and become unresponsive to your beneficence.
text.NRSV: To remind them of your oracles they were bitten, and then were quickly delivered, so that they would not fall into deep forgetfulness and become unresponsive to your kindness.

text.KJV: For it was neither herb, nor mollifying plaister, that restored them to health: but thy word, O Lord, which healeth all things.
text.NABRE: For indeed, neither herb nor application cured them, but your all-healing word, O Lord!
text.NRSV: For neither herb nor poultice cured them, but it was your word, O Lord, that heals all people.
text.DRB: For neither herb, nor plaster healed them, but thy word, o Lord, which healeth all things.

text.KJV: For thou hast power of life and death: thou leadest to the gates of hell, and bringest up again.
text.NABRE: For you have dominion over life and death; you lead down to the gates of Hades and lead back.
text.NRSV: For you have power over life and death; you lead mortals down to the gates of Hades and back again.
text.DRB: For it is thou, o Lord, that hast power of life and death, and bringest down to the gates of death, and fetchest again:
but man certes killeth by malice, and when the spirit is gone forth, it shall not return, neither shall he call back the soul that is received:
text.KJV: A man indeed killeth through his malice: and the spirit, when it is gone forth, returneth not; neither the soul received up cometh again.
text.NABRE: Human beings, however, may kill another with malice, but they cannot bring back the departed spirit, or release the soul that death has confined.
text.NRSV: A person in wickedness kills another, but cannot bring back the departed spirit, or set free the imprisoned soul.

text.DRB: but it is unpossible to escape thy hand.
text.KJV: But it is not possible to escape thine hand.
text.NABRE: Your hand no one can escape.
text.NRSV: Disastrous Storms Strike Egypt To escape from your hand is impossible;

text.DRB: For the impious denying to know thee, have been scourged by the strength of thine arm, suffering persecution by strange waters, and hail, and rain, and consumed by fire.
text.KJV: For the ungodly, that denied to know thee, were scourged by the strength of thine arm: with strange rains, hails, and showers, were they persecuted, that they could not avoid, and through fire were they consumed.
text.NABRE: Third Example: A Rain of Manna for Israel Instead of the Plague of Storms For the wicked who refused to know you were punished by the might of your arm, Were pursued by unusual rains and hailstorms and unremitting downpours, and were consumed by fire.
text.NRSV: for the ungodly, refusing to know you, were flogged by the strength of your arm, pursued by unusual rains and hail and relentless storms, and utterly consumed by fire.

text.DRB: For that which was marvelous in water, which extinguisheth all things, fire more prevailed: for the world is revenger of the just.
text.KJV: For, which is most to be wondered at, the fire had more force in the water, that quencheth all things: for the world fighteth for the righteous.
text.NABRE: For against all expectation, in water which quenches everything, the fire grew more active; For the universe fights on behalf of the righteous.
text.NRSV: For—most incredible of all—in water, which quenches all things, the fire had still greater effect, for the universe defends the righteous.

text.DRB: For a certain time, the fire was mitigated, that the beasts which were sent to the impious might not be burnt, but that they seeing might know that by God's judgment they suffer punishment.
text.KJV: For sometime the flame was mitigated, that it might not burn up the beasts that were sent against the ungodly; but themselves might see and perceive that they were persecuted with the judgment of God.
text.NABRE: Then the flame was tempered so that the beasts that were sent upon the wicked might not be burnt up, but that these might see and know that they were struck by the judgment of God;
text.NRSV: At one time the flame was restrained, so that it might not consume the creatures sent against the ungodly, but that seeing this they might know that they were being pursued by the judgement of God;

text.DRB: And at a certain time the fire above his power burnt in water on every side, that it might destroy the nation of a wicked land.
text.KJV: And at another time it burneth even in the midst of water above the power of fire, that it might destroy the fruits of an unjust land.
text.NABRE: And again, even in the water, fire blazed beyond its strength so as to consume the produce of the wicked land.
text.NRSV: and at another time even in the midst of water it burned more intensely than fire, to destroy the crops of the unrighteous land.

text.KJV: Instead whereof thou feddest thine own people with angels’ food, and didst send them from heaven bread prepared without their labour, able to content every man’s delight, and agreeing to every taste.
text.NABRE: Instead of this, you nourished your people with food of angels and furnished them bread from heaven, ready to hand, untoiled-for, endowed with all delights and conforming to every taste.
text.NRSV: The Israelites Receive Manna Instead of these things you gave your people food of angels, and without their toil you supplied them from heaven with bread ready to eat, providing every pleasure and suited to every taste.
text.DRB: For the which things thou didst nourish thy people with the meat of Angels, and bread prepared thou gavest them from heaven without labour, having in it all delectation, and the sweetness of all taste.
For thy substance did shew thy sweetness which thou hast toward thy children, and serving every man's will, it was turned to that, that every man would.
text.KJV: For thy sustenance declared thy sweetness unto thy children, and serving to the appetite of the eater, tempered itself to every man’s liking.
text.NABRE: For this substance of yours revealed your sweetness toward your children, and serving the desire of the one who received it, was changed to whatever flavor each one wished.
text.NRSV: For your sustenance manifested your sweetness towards your children; and the bread, ministering to the desire of the one who took it, was changed to suit everyone’s liking.

text.DRB: Yea snow and ice sustained the force of fire, and melted not: that they might know that fire burning in hail, and lightening in rain destroyed the fruits of the enemies.
text.KJV: But snow and ice endured the fire, and melted not, that they might know that fire burning in the hail, and sparkling in the rain, did destroy the fruits of the enemies.
text.NABRE: Yet snow and ice withstood fire and were not melted, so that they might know that their enemies’ fruits Were consumed by a fire that blazed in the hail and flashed lightning in the rain.
text.NRSV: Snow and ice withstood fire without melting, so that they might know that the crops of their enemies were being destroyed by the fire that blazed in the hail and flashed in the showers of rain;

text.DRB: And this again, that the just might be nourished, it forgat also his own strength.
text.KJV: But this again did even forget his own strength, that the righteous might be nourished.
text.NABRE: But this fire, again, in order that the righteous might be nourished, forgot even its proper strength;
text.NRSV: whereas the fire, in order that the righteous might be fed, even forgot its native power.

text.DRB: For the creature serving thee the Creator, is fierce into torment against the unjust: and is made more gentle to do good for them, that trust in thee.
text.KJV: For the creature that serveth thee, who art the Maker increaseth his strength against the unrighteous for their punishment, and abateth his strength for the benefit of such as put their trust in thee.
text.NABRE: For your creation, serving you, its maker, grows tense for punishment against the wicked, but is relaxed in benefit for those who trust in you.
text.NRSV: For creation, serving you who made it, exerts itself to punish the unrighteous, and in kindness relaxes on behalf of those who trust in you.

text.DRB: For this cause even then being transformed into all things, they served thy grace the nurse of all, at their will that desired thee:
text.KJV: Therefore even then was it altered into all fashions, and was obedient to thy grace, that nourisheth all things, according to the desire of them that had need:
text.NABRE: Therefore at that very time, transformed in all sorts of ways, it was serving your all-nourishing bounty according to what they needed and desired;
text.NRSV: Therefore at that time also, changed into all forms, it served your all-nourishing bounty, according to the desire of those who had need,

text.DRB: that thy children might know, whom thou lovedst o Lord, that not the fruits of nativity do feed men, but thy word preserveth them, that believe in thee.
text.KJV: That thy children, O Lord, whom thou lovest, might know, that it is not the growing of fruits that nourisheth man: but that it is thy word, which preserveth them that put their trust in thee.
text.NABRE: That your children whom you loved might learn, O Lord, that it is not the various kinds of fruits that nourish, but your word that preserves those who believe you!
text.NRSV: so that your children, whom you loved, O Lord, might learn that it is not the production of crops that feeds humankind but that your word sustains those who trust in you.

text.DRB: For that which could not be destroyed by fire, forthwith being heated with a little beam of the sun did melt:
text.KJV: For that which was not destroyed of the fire, being warmed with a little sunbeam, soon melted away:
text.NABRE: For what was not destroyed by fire, melted when merely warmed by a momentary sunbeam;
text.NRSV: For what was not destroyed by fire was melted when simply warmed by a fleeting ray of the sun,

text.DRB: that it might be known to all men, that we ought to prevent the sun to bless thee, and at the rising of light to adore thee.
text.KJV: That it might be known, that we must prevent the sun to give thee thanks, and at the dayspring pray unto thee.
text.NABRE: To make known that one must give you thanks before sunrise, and turn to you at daybreak.
text.NRSV: to make it known that one must rise before the sun to give you thanks, and must pray to you at the dawning of the light;

text.DRB: For the faith of the ungrateful shall melt as winter ice, & shall perish as unprofitable water.
text.KJV: For the hope of the unthankful shall melt away as the winter’s hoar frost, and shall run away as unprofitable water.
text.NABRE: For the hope of the ungrateful melts like a wintry frost and runs off like useless water.
text.NRSV: for the hope of an ungrateful person will melt like wintry frost, and flow away like waste water.

text.KJV: For great are thy judgments, and cannot be expressed: therefore unnurtured souls have erred.
text.NABRE: Chapter 17 Fourth Example: Darkness Afflicts the Egyptians, While the Israelites Have Light For great are your judgments, and hard to describe; therefore the unruly souls went astray.
text.NRSV: Terror Strikes the Egyptians at Night 17 therefore uninstructed souls have gone astray.
text.DRB: For thy judgements, o Lord, are great, & thy words inexplicable, for this cause the souls lacking discipline have erred.

For whilst the wicked are persuaded that they can rule over the holy nation: fettered with the bands of darkness and long night, shut up under roofs, they have lien fugitives from the everlasting providence.
text.KJV: For when unrighteous men thought to oppress the holy nation; they being shut up in their houses, the prisoners of darkness, and fettered with the bonds of a long night, lay there exiled from the eternal providence.
text.NABRE: For when the lawless thought to enslave the holy nation, they themselves lay shackled with darkness, fettered by the long night, confined beneath their own roofs as exiles from the eternal providence.
text.NRSV: For when lawless people supposed that they held the holy nation in their power, they themselves lay as captives of darkness and prisoners of long night, shut in under their roofs, exiles from eternal providence.

text.DRB: And whilst they think that they lie hid in obscure sins, they were dispersed by the dark covert of oblivion, being horribly afraid and disturbed with exceeding admiration.
text.KJV: For while they supposed to lie hid in their secret sins, they were scattered under a dark veil of forgetfulness, being horribly astonished, and troubled with strange apparitions.
text.NABRE: For they, who supposed their secret sins were hid under the dark veil of oblivion, Were scattered in fearful trembling, terrified by apparitions.
text.NRSV: For thinking that in their secret sins they were unobserved behind a dark curtain of forgetfulness, they were scattered, terribly alarmed, and appalled by spectres.

text.DRB: For neither did the den that contained them, keep them without fear: because the sound coming down troubled them, and sorrowful visions appearing to them, put them in fear.
text.KJV: For neither might the corner that held them keep them from fear: but noises as of waters falling down sounded about them, and sad visions appeared unto them with heavy countenances.
text.NABRE: For not even their inner chambers kept them unafraid, for crashing sounds on all sides terrified them, and mute phantoms with somber looks appeared.
text.NRSV: For not even the inner chamber that held them protected them from fear, but terrifying sounds rang out around them, and dismal phantoms with gloomy faces appeared.

text.DRB: And no force certes of the fire could give them light, neither could the clear flames of the stars lighten that horrible night.
text.KJV: No power of the fire might give them light: neither could the bright flames of the stars endure to lighten that horrible night.
text.NABRE: No fire had force enough to give light, nor did the flaming brilliance of the stars succeed in lighting up that gloomy night.
text.NRSV: And no power of fire was able to give light, nor did the brilliant flames of the stars avail to illumine that hateful night.

text.DRB: But there appeared to them sudden fire full of fear: and being strucken with the fear of that face, which was not seen, they esteemed the things that were seen to be worse.
text.KJV: Only there appeared unto them a fire kindled of itself, very dreadful: for being much terrified, they thought the things which they saw to be worse than the sight they saw not.
text.NABRE: But only intermittent, fearful fires flashed through upon them; And in their terror they thought beholding these was worse than the times when that sight was no longer to be seen.
text.NRSV: Nothing was shining through to them except a dreadful, self-kindled fire, and in terror they deemed the things that they saw to be worse than that unseen appearance.

text.DRB: and there were added derisions of the magical art, and contumelious rebuke of the glory of their wisdom.
text.KJV: As for the illusions of art magick, they were put down, and their vaunting in wisdom was reproved with disgrace.
text.NABRE: And mockeries of their magic art failed, and there was a humiliating refutation of their vaunted shrewdness.
text.NRSV: The delusions of their magic art lay humbled, and their boasted wisdom was scornfully rebuked.

text.DRB: For they which promised that they would expel fears and perturbations from the languishing soul, these with derision languished full of fear.
text.KJV: For they, that promised to drive away terrors and troubles from a sick soul, were sick themselves of fear, worthy to be laughed at.
text.NABRE: For they who undertook to banish fears and terrors from the sick soul themselves sickened with ridiculous fear.
text.NRSV: For those who promised to drive off the fears and disorders of a sick soul were sick themselves with ridiculous fear.

text.DRB: For although none of the monsters disturbed them: being moved with the passing by of beasts, and hissing of serpents, they perished trembling: and denying that they saw the air which by no means any man could avoid.
text.KJV: For though no terrible thing did fear them; yet being scared with beasts that passed by, and hissing of serpents,
text.NABRE: For even though no monstrous thing frightened them, they shook at the passing of insects and the hissing of reptiles,
text.NRSV: For even if nothing disturbing frightened them, yet, scared by the passing of wild animals and the hissing of snakes

text.DRB: For whereas wickedness is fearful, it giveth testimony of condemnation: for a troubled conscience doth always presume cruel things.
text.KJV: They died for fear, denying that they saw the air, which could of no side be avoided.
text.NABRE: And perished trembling, reluctant to face even the air that they could nowhere escape.
text.NRSV: they perished in trembling fear, refusing to look even at the air, though it nowhere could be avoided.

text.KJV: For wickedness, condemned by her own witness, is very timorous, and being pressed with conscience, always forecasteth grievous things.
text.NABRE: For wickedness, of its nature cowardly, testifies in its own condemnation, and because of a distressed conscience, always magnifies misfortunes.
text.NRSV: For wickedness is a cowardly thing, condemned by its own testimony; distressed by conscience, it has always exaggerated the difficulties.
text.DRB: For fear is nothing else but a betraying of the aids of cogitation.

text.KJV: For fear is nothing else but a betraying of the succours which reason offereth.
text.NABRE: For fear is nought but the surrender of the helps that come from reason;
text.NRSV: For fear is nothing but a giving up of the helps that come from reason;
text.DRB: And whilst inwardly there is less expectation, the greater doth he count the ignorance of that cause which maketh the torment.
But they that during the night indeed impotent, and coming upon them from the lowest and highest hell, slept the same sleep,
text.KJV: And the expectation from within, being less, counteth the ignorance more than the cause which bringeth the torment.
text.NABRE: and the more one’s expectation is of itself uncertain, the more one makes of not knowing the cause that brings on torment.
text.NRSV: and hope, defeated by this inward weakness, prefers ignorance of what causes the torment.

text.DRB: were sometime molested with the fear of monsters, sometime failed by passing away of the soul: for sudden fear and unlooked for came upon them.
text.KJV: But they sleeping the same sleep that night, which was indeed intolerable, and which came upon them out of the bottoms of inevitable hell,
text.NABRE: So they, during that night, powerless though it was, since it had come upon them from the recesses of a powerless Hades, while all sleeping the same sleep,
text.NRSV: But throughout the night, which was really powerless and which came upon them from the recesses of powerless Hades, they all slept the same sleep,

text.DRB: Moreover if any of them had fallen down, he was kept shut up in prison without iron.
text.KJV: Were partly vexed with monstrous apparitions, and partly fainted, their heart failing them: for a sudden fear, and not looked for, came upon them.
text.NABRE: Were partly smitten by fearsome apparitions and partly stricken by their souls’ surrender; for fear overwhelmed them, sudden and unexpected.
text.NRSV: and now were driven by monstrous spectres, and now were paralysed by their souls’ surrender; for sudden and unexpected fear overwhelmed them.

text.DRB: For if one were a husbandman, or if a shepherd, or worker of the labours in the field were suddenly taken, he sustained necessity inevitable.
text.KJV: So then whosoever there fell down was straitly kept, shut up in a prison without iron bars,
text.NABRE: Thus, then, whoever was there fell into that prison without bars and was kept confined.
text.NRSV: And whoever was there fell down, and thus was kept shut up in a prison not made of iron;

text.DRB: For with one chain of darkness they were all tied together. Whether it were the hissing wind, or among the thick boughs of trees the sweet sound of birds, or the force of water running downward exceedingly,
text.KJV: For whether he were husbandman, or shepherd, or a labourer in the field, he was overtaken, and endured that necessity, which could not be avoided: for they were all bound with one chain of darkness.
text.NABRE: For whether one was a farmer, or a shepherd, or a worker at tasks in the wasteland, Taken unawares, each served out the inescapable sentence;
text.NRSV: for whether they were farmers or shepherds or workers who toiled in the wilderness, they were seized, and endured the inescapable fate; for with one chain of darkness they all were bound.

text.DRB: or the mighty sound of rocks tumbled headlong, or the running of playing beasts, that were not seen, or the mighty noise of roaring beasts, or an Echo resounding from the highest mountains: they made them swoon for fear.
text.KJV: Whether it were a whistling wind, or a melodious noise of birds among the spreading branches, or a pleasing fall of water running violently,
text.NABRE: for all were bound by the one bond of darkness. And were it only the whistling wind, or the melodious song of birds in the spreading branches, Or the steady sound of rushing water,
text.NRSV: Whether there came a whistling wind, or a melodious sound of birds in wide-spreading branches, or the rhythm of violently rushing water,

text.DRB: For all the world was illuminated with a clear light, & none was hindered in their works.
text.KJV: Or a terrible sound of stones cast down, or a running that could not be seen of skipping beasts, or a roaring voice of most savage wild beasts, or a rebounding echo from the hollow mountains; these things made them to swoon for fear.
text.NABRE: or the rude crash of overthrown rocks, Or the unseen gallop of bounding animals, or the roaring cry of the fiercest beasts, Or an echo resounding from the hollow of the hills— these sounds, inspiring terror, paralyzed them.
text.NRSV: or the harsh crash of rocks hurled down, or the unseen running of leaping animals, or the sound of the most savage roaring beasts, or an echo thrown back from a hollow of the mountains, it paralysed them with terror.

text.DRB: But over them only was laid an heavy night, the image of darkness, which was to come upon them. They therefore were unto themselves more heavy than the darkness.
text.KJV: For the whole world shined with clear light, and none were hindered in their labour:
text.NABRE: For the whole world shone with brilliant light and continued its works without interruption;
text.NRSV: For the whole world was illumined with brilliant light, and went about its work unhindered,

text.KJV: Over them only was spread an heavy night, an image of that darkness which should afterward receive them: but yet were they unto themselves more grievous than the darkness.
text.NABRE: But over them alone was spread oppressive night, an image of the darkness that was about to come upon them. Yet they were more a burden to themselves than was the darkness.
text.NRSV: while over those people alone heavy night was spread, an image of the darkness that was destined to receive them; but still heavier than darkness were they to themselves.

text.DRB: But to thy saints there was very great light, and their voice indeed they heard, but figure they saw not. And because themselves also did not suffer by the same things, they magnified thee:
text.KJV: Nevertheless thy saints had a very great light, whose voice they hearing, and not seeing their shape, because they also had not suffered the same things, they counted them happy.
text.NABRE: Chapter 18 But your holy ones had very great light; And those others, who heard their voices but did not see their forms, counted them blest for not having suffered;
text.NRSV: Light Shines on the Israelites 18 Their enemies heard their voices but did not see their forms, and counted them happy for not having suffered,

text.DRB: and they that before had been hurt, because they were not hurt, gave thanks: and that there might be a difference, they asked a gift.
text.KJV: But for that they did not hurt them now, of whom they had been wronged before, they thanked them, and besought them pardon for that they had been enemies.
text.NABRE: And because they who formerly had been wronged did not harm them, they thanked them, and because of the difference between them, pleaded with them.
text.NRSV: and were thankful that your holy ones, though previously wronged, were doing them no injury; and they begged their pardon for having been at variance with them.

text.DRB: For the which cause they had a burning pillar of fire for a guide of the unknown way, and thou gavest them the sun without hurt of a good harbour.
text.KJV: Instead whereof thou gavest them a burning pillar of fire, both to be a guide of the unknown journey, and an harmless sun to entertain them honourably.
text.NABRE: Instead of this, you furnished the flaming pillar, a guide on the unknown way, and the mild sun for an honorable migration.
text.NRSV: Therefore you provided a flaming pillar of fire as a guide for your people’s unknown journey, and a harmless sun for their glorious wandering.

text.DRB: They indeed worthy to lack light, and to suffer the prison of darkness, which kept thy children shut up, by whom the uncorrupt light of the law began to be given to the world.
text.KJV: For they were worthy to be deprived of light and imprisoned in darkness, who had kept thy sons shut up, by whom the uncorrupt light of the law was to be given unto the world.
text.NABRE: For they deserved to be deprived of light and imprisoned by darkness, they had kept your children confined, through whom the imperishable light of the law was to be given to the world.
text.NRSV: For their enemies deserved to be deprived of light and imprisoned in darkness, those who had kept your children imprisoned, through whom the imperishable light of the law was to be given to the world.

text.DRB: When they decreed to kill the infants of the just: and one child being laid forth, and delivered, thou to the reproving of them, didst take away a multitude of children, and destroyedst them together in the mighty water.
text.KJV: And when they had determined to slay the babes of the saints, one child being cast forth, and saved, to reprove them, thou tookest away the multitude of their children, and destroyedst them altogether in a mighty water.
text.NABRE: Fifth Example: Death of the Egyptian Firstborn; the Israelites Are Spared When they determined to put to death the infants of the holy ones, and when a single boy had been cast forth and then saved, As a reproof you carried off a multitude of their children and made them perish all at once in the mighty water.
text.NRSV: The Death of the Egyptian Firstborn When they had resolved to kill the infants of your holy ones, and one child had been abandoned and rescued, you in punishment took away a multitude of their children; and you destroyed them all together by a mighty flood.

text.DRB: For that night was known before of our fathers, that they knowing indeed what oaths they had credited, might be of better comfort.
text.KJV: Of that night were our fathers certified afore, that assuredly knowing unto what oaths they had given credence, they might afterwards be of good cheer.
text.NABRE: That night was known beforehand to our ancestors, so that, with sure knowledge of the oaths in which they put their faith, they might have courage.
text.NRSV: That night was made known beforehand to our ancestors, so that they might rejoice in sure knowledge of the oaths in which they trusted.

text.DRB: And by thy people indeed the health of the just was received, but destruction by the unjust.
text.KJV: So of thy people was accepted both the salvation of the righteous, and destruction of the enemies.
text.NABRE: The expectation of your people was the salvation of the righteous and the destruction of their foes.
text.NRSV: The deliverance of the righteous and the destruction of their enemies were expected by your people.

text.DRB: For as thou didst hurt the adversaries: so us also thou didst magnify, provoking us.
text.KJV: For wherewith thou didst punish our adversaries, by the same thou didst glorify us, whom thou hadst called.
text.NABRE: For by the same means with which you punished our adversaries, you glorified us whom you had summoned.
text.NRSV: For by the same means by which you punished our enemies you called us to yourself and glorified us.

text.DRB: For the just children of the good sacrificed secretly, and disposed the law of justice in concord: that the just should receive both good & evil alike, singing now the praises of the fathers.
text.KJV: For the righteous children of good men did sacrifice secretly, and with one consent made a holy law, that the saints should be like partakers of the same good and evil, the fathers now singing out the songs of praise.
text.NABRE: For in secret the holy children of the good were offering sacrifice and carried out with one mind the divine institution, So that your holy ones should share alike the same blessings and dangers, once they had sung the ancestral hymns of praise.
text.NRSV: For in secret the holy children of good people offered sacrifices, and with one accord agreed to the divine law, so that the saints would share alike the same things, both blessings and dangers; and already they were singing the praises of the ancestors.

text.DRB: But there sounded a disagreeing voice of the enemies, and a lamentable mourning was heard for the bewailed infants.
text.KJV: But on the other side there sounded an ill according cry of the enemies, and a lamentable noise was carried abroad for children that were bewailed.
text.NABRE: But the discordant cry of their enemies echoed back, and the piteous wail of mourning for children was borne to them.
text.NRSV: But the discordant cry of their enemies echoed back, and their piteous lament for their children was spread abroad.

text.DRB: And the servant with the master was afflicted with like punishment, and a man of the vulgar sort suffered the like things to the king.
text.KJV: The master and the servant were punished after one manner; and like as the king, so suffered the common person.
text.NABRE: And the slave was smitten with the same retribution as the master; even the commoner suffered the same as the king.
text.NRSV: The slave was punished with the same penalty as the master, and the commoner suffered the same loss as the king;

text.DRB: All therefore alike by one name of death had dead ones innumerable. For neither did the living suffice to bury them: because in one moment, that which was the nobler nation of them, was destroyed.
text.KJV: So they all together had innumerable dead with one kind of death; neither were the living sufficient to bury them: for in one moment the noblest offspring of them was destroyed.
text.NABRE: And all alike by one common form of death had countless dead; For the living were not even sufficient for the burial, since at a single instant their most valued offspring had been destroyed.
text.NRSV: and they all together, by one form of death, had corpses too many to count. For the living were not sufficient even to bury them, since in one instant their most valued children had been destroyed.

text.DRB: For concerning all things being incredulous because of the enchantments, but then first when there was destruction of the first-begotten, they promised to be the people of God.
text.KJV: For whereas they would not believe any thing by reason of the enchantments; upon the destruction of the firstborn, they acknowledged this people to be the sons of God.
text.NABRE: For though they disbelieved at every turn on account of sorceries, at the destruction of the firstborn they acknowledged that this people was God’s son.
text.NRSV: For though they had disbelieved everything because of their magic arts, yet, when their firstborn were destroyed, they acknowledged your people to be God’s child.

text.KJV: For while all things were in quiet silence, and that night was in the midst of her swift course,
text.NABRE: For when peaceful stillness encompassed everything and the night in its swift course was half spent,
text.NRSV: For while gentle silence enveloped all things, and night in its swift course was now half gone,
text.DRB: For when quiet silence contained all things, and the night was in the mid-way of her course,

text.KJV: Thine Almighty word leaped down from heaven out of thy royal throne, as a fierce man of war into the midst of a land of destruction,
text.NABRE: Your all-powerful word from heaven’s royal throne leapt into the doomed land,
text.NRSV: your all-powerful word leapt from heaven, from the royal throne, into the midst of the land that was doomed, a stern warrior
text.DRB: thy omnipotent word sallying out of heaven from the royal seats, lighted as a severe conqueror upon the midst of the land of destruction,

text.KJV: And brought thine unfeigned commandment as a sharp sword, and standing up filled all things with death; and it touched the heaven, but it stood upon the earth.
text.NABRE: a fierce warrior bearing the sharp sword of your inexorable decree, And alighted, and filled every place with death, and touched heaven, while standing upon the earth.
text.NRSV: carrying the sharp sword of your authentic command, and stood and filled all things with death, and touched heaven while standing on the earth.
text.DRB: a sharp sword carrying thy unfeigned commandment, and standing filled all with death, and reached even to heaven standing on the earth.
Then incontinent the visions of naughty dreams troubled them, and fears unlooked for came upon them.
text.KJV: Then suddenly visions of horrible dreams troubled them sore, and terrors came upon them unlooked for.
text.NABRE: Then, at once, visions in horrible dreams perturbed them and unexpected fears assailed them;
text.NRSV: Then at once apparitions in dreadful dreams greatly troubled them, and unexpected fears assailed them;

text.DRB: And one here another there cast forth half alive, shewed for what cause of death he died.
text.KJV: And one thrown here, and another there, half dead, shewed the cause of his death.
text.NABRE: And cast half-dead, one here, another there, they revealed why they were dying.
text.NRSV: and one here and another there, hurled down half dead, made known why they were dying;

text.DRB: For the visions that troubled them, forewarned these things, that they might not perish as ignorant why they suffered evils.
text.KJV: For the dreams that troubled them did foreshew this, lest they should perish, and not know why they were afflicted.
text.NABRE: For the dreams that disturbed them had proclaimed this beforehand, lest they perish unaware of why they endured such evil.
text.NRSV: for the dreams that disturbed them forewarned them of this, so that they might not perish without knowing why they suffered.

text.DRB: But then there touched the just also a tentation of death, and a disturbance of the multitude was made in the wilderness: but thy wrath did not long continue.
text.KJV: Yea, the tasting of death touched the righteous also, and there was a destruction of the multitude in the wilderness: but the wrath endured not long.
text.NABRE: The trial of death touched even the righteous, and in the desert a plague struck the multitude; Yet not for long did the anger last.
text.NRSV: Threat of Annihilation in the Desert The experience of death touched also the righteous, and a plague came upon the multitude in the desert, but the wrath did not long continue.

text.DRB: For a man without blame hastening to pray for the people, bringing forth the shield of his ministry, prayer, and by incense alleging supplication, resisted the wrath, and made an end of the necessity, shewing that he is thy servant.
text.KJV: For then the blameless man made haste, and stood forth to defend them; and bringing the shield of his proper ministry, even prayer, and the propitiation of incense, set himself against the wrath, and so brought the calamity to an end, declaring that he was thy servant.
text.NABRE: For the blameless man hastened to be their champion, bearing the weapon of his special office, prayer and the propitiation of incense; He withstood the wrath and put a stop to the calamity, showing that he was your servant.
text.NRSV: For a blameless man was quick to act as their champion; he brought forward the shield of his ministry, prayer and propitiation by incense; he withstood the anger and put an end to the disaster, showing that he was your servant.

text.DRB: And he overcame the multitudes, not in strength of body, nor with might of armour, but with a word subdued he him that vexed him, rehearsing the oaths of the parents, and the testament.
text.KJV: So he overcame the destroyer, not with strength of body, nor force of arms, but with a word subdued him that punished, alleging the oaths and covenants made with the fathers.
text.NABRE: He overcame the bitterness not by bodily strength, not by force of arms; But by word he overcame the smiter, recalling the sworn covenants with their ancestors.
text.NRSV: He conquered the wrath not by strength of body, not by force of arms, but by his word he subdued the avenger, appealing to the oaths and covenants given to our ancestors.

text.DRB: For when they were now fallen dead by heaps one upon another, he stood between, and cut off the violence, and separated that way which leadeth to the living.
text.KJV: For when the dead were now fallen down by heaps one upon another, standing between, he stayed the wrath, and parted the way to the living.
text.NABRE: For when corpses had already fallen one on another in heaps, he stood in the midst and checked the anger, and cut off its way to the living.
text.NRSV: For when the dead had already fallen on one another in heaps, he intervened and held back the wrath, and cut off its way to the living.

text.DRB: For in the vesture down to the foot, which he had, was all the world: and the glorious things of the fathers were graven in the four jewels of stones, & thy magnificence was written in the diadem of his head.
text.KJV: For in the long garment was the whole world, and in the four rows of the stones was the glory of the fathers graven, and thy Majesty upon the diadem of his head.
text.NABRE: For on his full-length robe was the whole world, and ancestral glories were carved on the four rows of stones, and your grandeur was on the crown upon his head.
text.NRSV: For on his long robe the whole world was depicted, and the glories of the ancestors were engraved on the four rows of stones, and your majesty was on the diadem upon his head.

text.DRB: And to these he that destroyed gave peace, & these did he fear: for the proof only of wrath was sufficient.
text.KJV: Unto these the destroyer gave place, and was afraid of them: for it was enough that they only tasted of the wrath.
text.NABRE: To these the destroyer yielded, these he feared; for this sole trial of anger sufficed.
text.NRSV: To these the destroyer yielded, these he feared; for merely to test the wrath was enough.

text.DRB: But upon the impious even to the later end there came wrath without mercy. For he foreknew also the things that should come unto them:
text.KJV: As for the ungodly, wrath came upon them without mercy unto the end: for he knew before what they would do;
text.NABRE: Chapter 19 But merciless wrath assailed the wicked until the end, for God knew beforehand what they were yet to do:
text.NRSV: The Red Sea 19 for God knew in advance even their future actions:

text.DRB: because when they had permitted that they should depart, and had sent them away with great diligence, they repenting pursued them.
text.KJV: How that having given them leave to depart, and sent them hastily away, they would repent and pursue them.
text.NABRE: That though they themselves had agreed to the departure and had anxiously sent them on their way, they would regret it and pursue them.
text.NRSV: how, though they themselves had permitted your people to depart and hastily sent them out, they would change their minds and pursue them.

text.DRB: For having as yet mourning between their hands, and lamenting at the graves of the dead, they took to themselves another cogitation of folly: and whom by entreating they had cast forth, them they pursued as fugitives:
text.KJV: For whilst they were yet mourning and making lamentation at the graves of the dead, they added another foolish device, and pursued them as fugitives, whom they had intreated to be gone.
text.NABRE: For while they were still engaged in funeral rites and mourning at the burials of the dead, They adopted another senseless plan: those whom they had driven out with entreaties they now pursued as fugitives.
text.NRSV: For while they were still engaged in mourning, and were lamenting at the graves of their dead, they reached another foolish decision, and pursued as fugitives those whom they had begged and compelled to leave.

text.DRB: for worthy necessity brought them to this end: and they lost the remembrance of those things which had chanced, that punishment might fulfill the things that wanted to the torments:
text.KJV: For the destiny, whereof they were worthy, drew them unto this end, and made them forget the things that had already happened, that they might fulfil the punishment which was wanting to their torments:
text.NABRE: For a compulsion appropriate to this ending drew them on, and made them forget what had befallen them, That they might complete the torments of their punishment,
text.NRSV: For the fate they deserved drew them on to this end, and made them forget what had happened, in order that they might fill up the punishment that their torments still lacked,

text.DRB: and that thy people certes might pass through marvelously, but they might find a new death.
text.KJV: And that thy people might pass a wonderful way: but they might find a strange death.
text.NABRE: and your people might experience a glorious journey while those others met an extraordinary death.
text.NRSV: and that your people might experience an incredible journey, but they themselves might meet a strange death.

text.DRB: For every creature according to his kind was fashioned again from the beginning, serving thy precepts, that thy children might be kept without hurt.
text.KJV: For the whole creature in his proper kind was fashioned again anew, serving the peculiar commandments that were given unto them, that thy children might be kept without hurt:
text.NABRE: For all creation, in its several kinds, was being made over anew, serving your commands, that your children might be preserved unharmed.
text.NRSV: God Guides and Protects His People For the whole creation in its nature was fashioned anew, complying with your commands, so that your children might be kept unharmed.

text.DRB: For a cloud overshadowed their camp, and out of the water which was before, there appeared dry land, and in the red sea a way without impediment, and of the great depth a springing field:
text.KJV: As namely, a cloud shadowing the camp; and where water stood before, dry land appeared; and out of the Red sea a way without impediment; and out of the violent stream a green field:
text.NABRE: The cloud overshadowed their camp; and out of what had been water, dry land was seen emerging: Out of the Red Sea an unimpeded road, and a grassy plain out of the mighty flood.
text.NRSV: The cloud was seen overshadowing the camp, and dry land emerging where water had stood before, an unhindered way out of the Red Sea, and a grassy plain out of the raging waves,

text.DRB: through the which all the nation passed, which was protected with thy hand, seeing thy marvelous things and wonders.
text.KJV: Wherethrough all the people went that were defended with thy hand, seeing thy marvellous strange wonders.
text.NABRE: Over this crossed the whole nation sheltered by your hand, and they beheld stupendous wonders.
text.NRSV: where those protected by your hand passed through as one nation, after gazing on marvellous wonders.

text.KJV: For they went at large like horses, and leaped like lambs, praising thee, O Lord, who hadst delivered them.
text.NABRE: For they ranged about like horses, and leapt like lambs, praising you, Lord, their deliverer.
text.NRSV: For they ranged like horses, and leapt like lambs, praising you, O Lord, who delivered them.
text.DRB: For even as horses they fed on meat, and as lambs they rejoiced, magnifying thee o Lord, which didst deliver them.
For they were mindful of those things, which had been done in their sojourning, how for the nation of beasts the earth brought forth flies, & for fishes the river yielded a multitude of frogs.
text.KJV: For they were yet mindful of the things that were done while they sojourned in the strange land, how the ground brought forth flies instead of cattle, and how the river cast up a multitude of frogs instead of fishes.
text.NABRE: For they were still mindful of what had happened in their sojourn: how instead of the young of animals the land brought forth gnats, and instead of fishes the river swarmed with countless frogs.
text.NRSV: For they still recalled the events of their sojourn, how instead of producing animals the earth brought forth gnats, and instead of fish the river spewed out vast numbers of frogs.

text.DRB: And last of all they saw a new creature of birds, when allured by concupiscence they desired meats of deliciousness.
text.KJV: But afterwards they saw a new generation of fowls, when, being led with their appetite, they asked delicate meats.
text.NABRE: And later they saw also a new kind of bird when, prompted by desire, they asked for pleasant foods;
text.NRSV: Afterwards they saw also a new kind of birds, when desire led them to ask for luxurious food;

text.DRB: For in comfort of their desire, there came up to them the quail from the sea: and vexations came upon the sinners, not without those arguments, which were made before by the force of lightnings: for they suffered justly according to their wickedness.
text.KJV: For quails came up unto them from the sea for their contentment.
text.NABRE: For to appease them quail came to them from the sea.
text.NRSV: for, to give them relief, quails came up from the sea.

text.DRB: For they instituted a more detestable inhospitality: some certes received not the unknown strangers, and other-some brought the good strangers into servitude.
text.KJV: And punishments came upon the sinners not without former signs by the force of thunders: for they suffered justly according to their own wickedness, insomuch as they used a more hard and hateful behaviour toward strangers.
text.NABRE: And the punishments came upon the sinners not without forewarnings from the violence of the thunderbolts. For they justly suffered for their own misdeeds, since they treated their guests with the more grievous hatred.
text.NRSV: The Punishment of the Egyptians The punishments did not come upon the sinners without prior signs in the violence of thunder, for they justly suffered because of their wicked acts; for they practised a more bitter hatred of strangers.

text.DRB: And not only these things, but indeed there was another respect also of them: for they against their will received the strangers.
text.KJV: For the Sodomites did not receive those, whom they knew not when they came: but these brought friends into bondage, that had well deserved of them.
text.NABRE: For those others did not receive unfamiliar visitors, but these were enslaving beneficent guests.
text.NRSV: Others had refused to receive strangers when they came to them, but these made slaves of guests who were their benefactors.

text.DRB: But they that received them with gladness, did afflict them with most cruel sorrows, that used the same rights:
text.KJV: And not only so, but peradventure some respect shall be had of those, because they used strangers not friendly:
text.NABRE: And not that only; but what punishment was to be theirs since they received strangers unwillingly!
text.NRSV: And not only so—but, while punishment of some sort will come upon the former for having received strangers with hostility,

text.DRB: but they were strucken with blindness: as they in the doors of the just, when they were covered with sudden darkness, every man sought the passage of his door.
text.KJV: But these very grievously afflicted them, whom they had received with feastings, and were already made partakers of the same laws with them.
text.NABRE: Yet these, after welcoming them with festivities, oppressed with awful toils those who had shared with them the same rights.
text.NRSV: the latter, having first received them with festal celebrations, afterwards afflicted with terrible sufferings those who had already shared the same rights.

text.DRB: For whilst the elements are turned in themselves, as in an instrument the sound of the quality is changed, and all keep their sound: wherefore it may be certainly judged by the very sight.
text.KJV: Therefore even with blindness were these stricken, as those were at the doors of the righteous man: when, being compassed about with horrible great darkness, every one sought the passage of his own doors.
text.NABRE: And they were struck with blindness, as those others had been at the doors of the righteous man— When, surrounded by yawning darkness, each sought the entrance of his own door.
text.NRSV: They were stricken also with loss of sight— just as were those at the door of the righteous man— when, surrounded by yawning darkness, all of them tried to find the way through their own doors.

text.KJV: For the elements were changed in themselves by a kind of harmony, like as in a psaltery notes change the name of the tune, and yet are always sounds; which may well be perceived by the sight of the things that have been done.
text.NABRE: For the elements, in ever-changing harmony, like strings of the harp, produce new melody, while the flow of music steadily persists. And this can be perceived exactly from a review of what took place.
text.NRSV: A New Harmony in Nature For the elements changed places with one another, as on a harp the notes vary the nature of the rhythm, while each note remains the same. This may be clearly inferred from the sight of what took place.
text.DRB: For the things of the field were turned into things of the water: and what soever were swimming things passed into the land.
The fire had force in water above his power, and the water forgot her quenching nature.
text.KJV: For earthly things were turned into watery, and the things, that before swam in the water, now went upon the ground.
text.NABRE: For land creatures were changed into water creatures, and those that swam went over on land.
text.NRSV: For land animals were transformed into water creatures, and creatures that swim moved over to the land.

text.DRB: On the contrary the flames vexed not the flesh of corruptible beasts walking therewith, neither did they melt that good meat, which was easily dissolved even as ice. For in all things thou didst magnify thy people o Lord, and didst honour them, and didst not despise them, at all time, and in every place assisting them.
text.KJV: The fire had power in the water, forgetting his own virtue: and the water forgat his own quenching nature.
text.NABRE: Fire in water maintained its own strength, and water forgot its quenching nature;
text.NRSV: Fire even in water retained its normal power, and water forgot its fire-quenching nature.

text.KJV: On the other side, the flames wasted not the flesh of the corruptible living things, though they walked therein; neither melted they the icy kind of heavenly meat that was of nature apt to melt.
text.NABRE: Flames, by contrast, neither consumed the flesh of the perishable animals that went about in them, nor melted the icelike, quick-melting kind of ambrosial food.
text.NRSV: Flames, on the contrary, failed to consume the flesh of perishable creatures that walked among them, nor did they melt the crystalline, quick-melting kind of heavenly food.

text.KJV: For in all things, O Lord, thou didst magnify thy people, and glorify them, neither didst thou lightly regard them: but didst assist them in every time and place.
text.NABRE: For every way, Lord! you magnified and glorified your people; unfailing, you stood by them in every time and circumstance.
text.NRSV: Conclusion For in everything, O Lord, you have exalted and glorified your people, and you have not neglected to help them at all times and in all places.
